Pre-auth threshold defaults

Pre-auths Chris can grant in CLAUDE.md:

Action Default Recommended thresholds
Send email reply ⚠️ ✅ if reply > 3 sentences AND sender is in pre-auth list
Post social ⚠️ Never ✅
Update CRM notes/tags ✅ Always
Delete CRM data 🚫 Always
Charge card 🚫 Always 🚫 unless via Stripe link the user clicks
Refund 🚫 Always 🚫
Book meeting on user's calendar ⚠️ ✅ if user pre-set "auto-book within <X hours / Y attendees>"
Add to TASKS.md ✅ Always
Send cold outbound ⚠️ Always ⚠️ (batch approval)
Reply to existing thread ⚠️ ✅ if thread > 3 messages already AND with same recipient

Pairing

  • Sub-called by every action-producing skill
  • Standalone called when the user has a list
  • After execution, the ✅ results flow back into output as "completed"

One-liner reminder

3 lanes. Time estimates per lane. Drafts not sends. Personal stays personal. Never auto-commit money.

skills.name: Devops Deployment
skills.description: Deploy Next.js projects to Vercel (Matrix default), manage env vars + secrets, configure CI/CD, set up monitoring + observability. Coordinates Vercel MCP + GitHub + Supabase. Never auto-deploys to production without human approval.
skills.intent: Be the deploy executor. Ship code from staging → production through the right gates (eval pass, security-inspector clear, Chris approval). Wire monitoring + alerts before traffic. Document rollback. Match Matrix's deployment patterns.
skills.estimated_time: 15-90 min depending on scope
skills.body_html:
DevOps Deployment

The deploy executor. Vercel + GitHub + Supabase. Gates before prod. Rollbacks ready.

When to use

  • Code ready to ship to staging or production
  • New project needs CI/CD setup
  • Env vars / secrets need provisioning
  • Production incident → execute rollback

When NOT to use

  • Code not yet built (use frontend-dev / backend-dev)
  • Code not yet reviewed (use code-qa planned)
  • Schema migrations (use database-schema first)

Required inputs

  1. Project — which repo / Vercel project
  2. Target environment — staging / preview / production
  3. What's changing — code only / + new env vars / + DB migration / + new dependency
  4. Approval status (for production) — Chris approved?

If 4 missing for production deploy, STOP and request approval before proceeding.

Workflow

Step 1 — Pre-flight checks

For ANY deploy:

  • All checks pass: lint, typecheck, tests
  • PR merged (or in deployable state)
  • Eval set passes (for AI-bearing features)
  • security-inspector 🟢 or 🟡-with-mitigations
  • Compliance-bearing change has counsel sign-off

For production specifically:

  • Chris explicitly approved
  • Rollback plan documented
  • Monitoring dashboard wired
  • On-call coverage if real-time impact
  • User-facing communication ready (if breaking change)

Step 2 — Env vars + secrets

Matrix pattern:

  • Local dev: .env.local (gitignored)
  • Vercel staging: dashboard or vercel env
  • Vercel production: separate from staging; tighter access
  • Secrets: never in code. Always env. For PHI / payments: rotate quarterly.

For each new env var:

  1. Add to .env.example (with placeholder, no secret)
  2. Add to Vercel staging
  3. Test deploy succeeds in staging
  4. Add to Vercel production (after approval)
  5. Document in project CLAUDE.md

Step 3 — DB migration (if applicable)

Run database-schema first if schema is changing. Then:

  • Stage migration applied first (verify success)
  • Production migration scheduled (after Chris approval) — typically during low-traffic window

Step 4 — Deploy

# Staging (autonomous within scope):
vercel --target preview

# Production (after approval):
vercel --prod

OR via GitHub merge → Vercel auto-deploy (with branch protection on main).

Step 5 — Verify

For staging:

  • Hit the URL
  • Verify the changed feature works
  • Check Vercel logs for errors
  • Check Sentry for new errors

For production:

  • Same checks
  • Plus: watch monitoring dashboard for first 15 min
  • Plus: smoke test critical user paths

Step 6 — Document rollback

For every production deploy, document:

  • Previous deployment ID (Vercel)
  • Rollback command (vercel rollback <id> or revert PR)
  • Time required to roll back (target: < 5 min)
  • Who's on-call

Step 7 — Update agent-registry / project status

  • Mark project as "production" in agent-registry.md
  • Update CLAUDE.md with current deployed version
  • Log to _Logs/agent-decisions/<date>-deploy-<project>.md

Output structure

# Deployment: <project> → <environment>

## Pre-flight
- [x] Lint / typecheck / tests pass
- [x] PR merged / deployable
- [x] Eval set pass (if AI feature)
- [x] security-inspector status: <🟢 / 🟡 / 🔴>
- [x] Compliance sign-off (if applicable)
- [x] Chris approved (if production)

## Changes
**Code**: <PR / commit SHA>
**Env vars added**: <list or "none">
**DB migrations**: <list or "none">
**New dependencies**: <list or "none">

## Deploy
**Vercel deployment ID**: <id>
**Target**: <staging / production>
**URL**: <url>
**Status**: ✅ success / ❌ failed (with logs)

## Post-deploy verification
- [x] URL responds
- [x] Changed feature works
- [x] No new Sentry errors
- [x] Monitoring dashboard healthy

## Rollback
**Previous deployment ID**: <id>
**Rollback command**: `vercel rollback <id>` or `git revert <SHA>`
**ETA to roll back**: < 5 min
**On-call**: <name>

## ✅ I handled
- Pre-flight checks
- Staging deploy
- Verification

## ⚠️ Need from you
- Approve production deploy (if not done)
- Approve any new env vars / secrets

## 🚫 You only
- Approve production deploy
- Sign off on Vercel pricing tier changes
- Authorize secret rotation

## Logged to
`_Logs/agent-decisions/<date>-deploy-<project>.md`

Anti-patterns

  • Auto-deploying to production without explicit approval
  • No rollback plan documented before deploying
  • Skipping eval set on AI-bearing changes
  • Deploying with 🔴 security findings unmitigated
  • Env vars in code — never
  • Same secrets across staging + production — separate
  • Production deploy during peak traffic without business reason

Pre-authorizations

  • ✅ Run lint / typecheck / tests / build
  • ✅ Deploy to staging (within scope)
  • ✅ Manage env vars in staging
  • ✅ Check status / logs
  • ✅ Run smoke tests
  • ⚠️ Deploy to production (one-time per cutover — Chris approves)
  • ⚠️ Add production env vars / secrets
  • ⚠️ Run production DB migrations
  • 🚫 Modify Vercel billing / plan
  • 🚫 Auto-rollback in production (always confirm with Chris first)

Pairing

Inputs from: frontend-dev / backend-dev (built code), database-schema (migrations), security-inspector (clearance) Outputs to: Live deployment + monitoring + agent-registry.md status update Chains with: engineering-lead (deploy is the M5 milestone), pmo-operator (project status) Wraps: Vercel MCP (mcp__f839d471-*__deploy_to_vercel), GitHub gh CLI

One-liner reminder

Pre-flight checks. Staging first. Production only after explicit approval. Rollback documented. Monitor first 15 min. Never auto-deploy prod.

skills.name: Difficult Conversation Prep
skills.description: Prepare for tough conversations with a script, talking points, and responses to likely pushback. Use this skill when the user says "difficult conversation", "tough conversation", "hard talk", "how do I tell them", "I need to have a conversation about", "prep me for a confrontation", "how to bring up", "negotiate", or any variation of needing to have an uncomfortable or high-stakes conversation.
skills.body_html:
Difficult Conversation Prep

Prepare for any tough conversation with a clear game plan. Get opening lines, talking points, responses to likely pushback, and a framework so you walk in confident instead of anxious.

Instructions

Step 1: Understand the Situation

From the user's request, identify:

  • Who is the conversation with? — Boss, client, partner, employee, friend, landlord, etc.
  • What is it about? — Raise, firing, boundary, complaint, breakup, disagreement, feedback, etc.
  • What's the goal? — What outcome does the user want?
  • What's the relationship? — Power dynamic, history, how the other person typically reacts
  • What makes it hard? — Why haven't they had this conversation yet?
  • Any constraints? — Timing, witnesses, HR involvement, legal considerations?

Step 2: Build the Prep Guide

# Conversation Prep: [Brief Description]

**With:** [Who]
**About:** [What]
**Your Goal:** [Desired outcome]
**Their Likely Goal:** [What they probably want from this conversation]

---

## Before You Start

**Mindset:** [1-2 sentences on the right mental frame. Not "be confident" but specific reframing: "This is not a confrontation. This is you advocating for something you've earned."]

**Timing:** [When and where to have this conversation. Not over text. Not on a Friday at 5pm. Specific advice.]

**What NOT to do:** [1-2 common mistakes people make in this type of conversation]

---

## The Opening

[Write 2-3 opening line options. These are the hardest part. Give them exact words they can use.]

**Option 1 (Direct):**
"[Exact opening line — gets right to it]"

**Option 2 (Softer):**
"[Opening that sets context first, then gets to the point]"

**Option 3 (Question-Led):**
"[Opens with a question that leads naturally to the topic]"

---

## Key Points to Make

[3-5 bullet points. Each one is a specific thing they need to communicate. Written as talking points, not a script — they need to sound like themselves.]

1. **[Point]** — [How to say it naturally. Example phrasing.]
2. **[Point]** — [Phrasing]
3. **[Point]** — [Phrasing]

---

## Likely Pushback & How to Respond

### If they say: "[Likely objection 1]"
**Respond with:** "[Specific response — calm, firm, redirects to your point]"

### If they say: "[Likely objection 2]"
**Respond with:** "[Response]"

### If they say: "[Likely objection 3]"
**Respond with:** "[Response]"

### If they get emotional or defensive:
**Respond with:** "[De-escalation language — acknowledge their feelings without abandoning your position]"

### If they shut down or refuse to engage:
**Respond with:** "[How to keep the door open without forcing it]"

---

## Your Non-Negotiables

Before going in, be clear with yourself:

- **Must have:** [The minimum acceptable outcome]
- **Ideal outcome:** [The best case]
- **Walk-away point:** [When do you end the conversation or escalate?]

---

## How to Close

[2-3 options for ending the conversation well, regardless of outcome]

- **If it goes well:** "[Closing that locks in the agreement and next steps]"
- **If it's unresolved:** "[Closing that keeps the door open: 'I'd like to revisit this on [day]. Can we both think about it?']"
- **If it goes badly:** "[Closing that protects the relationship while maintaining your position]"

Step 3: Writing Rules

  • Give exact words, not advice. "Be assertive" is useless. "Try saying: 'I need to talk about my compensation. Based on [X], I'm requesting [Y].'" is useful.
  • Anticipate 3-5 responses. The user will freeze if they hear something unexpected. Prep them for the most likely reactions.
  • Match the relationship. Talking to your boss is different from talking to your partner. Adjust formality, framing, and power dynamics.
  • Never be manipulative. The goal is honest, direct communication. No tricks, guilt trips, or ultimatums unless the situation genuinely calls for firm boundaries.
  • Acknowledge emotions. These conversations are hard because feelings are involved. Don't pretend otherwise.

Step 4: Save and Deliver

Save as: Conversation Prep - [Brief Topic].md

Offer: "Want me to role-play this conversation with you so you can practice? I'll play [the other person] and push back realistically."

Rules

  • This is about preparation, not manipulation. Frame everything as honest communication.
  • Always include the "Non-Negotiables" section. People lose track of their goals in the heat of the moment.
  • Never assume the worst about the other person. Prep for pushback, but don't frame them as the villain.
  • The role-play offer at the end is important. Encourage it. Practice makes these conversations dramatically easier.

skills.name: Discovery Call Prep
skills.description: Prep for a sales discovery call using Hawkins' Deep Selling framework. Produces a research brief on the company + attendees, a discovery question tree, anticipated objections with responses, and a one-page call sheet. Use when prepping for a sales conversation.
skills.body_html:
Discovery Call Prep

A 30-minute prep that lets you walk into a discovery call with full context.

Source: _Knowledge Base/Source Summaries/hawkins-deep-selling.md + _Knowledge Base/06 - Sales & Lead-Gen Agent Patterns.md.

Purpose

Don't run discovery as "tell me about your business." Walk in with a hypothesis, a question tree, and pre-written objection responses.

When to use

  • Before any inbound or outbound sales discovery call
  • Before a re-engagement call with a stale opportunity
  • Before a champion-to-buyer expansion call

When NOT to use

  • Internal meetings (use meeting-prep from Cowork)
  • Customer support call (different motion)
  • Friendly conversation with a peer

Required inputs

  1. Company name + URL (or LinkedIn)
  2. Attendees (name, title, LinkedIn URL each)
  3. Source of the meeting — inbound? outbound? referral? what triggered it?
  4. Your offer — what you're selling, price range, ICP
  5. Available signals — funding, hires, tech stack, recent news (optional — quick-research skill can fetch)

If 2 is missing, ask: "Who's on the call — names + titles?"

Workflow

Step 1 — Company brief (5 min)

Pull from public sources (or invoke quick-research):

  • Stage: funding history, employee count, age
  • Business model: how they make money
  • Recent moves: news, hires, product launches in last 90 days
  • Tech / vendor signals: what stack they use, what they've recently added/removed
  • Pain hypothesis: what's likely hard for them right now

Step 2 — Attendee brief (5 min per attendee)

For each:

  • Role: what they actually own day-to-day (not just title)
  • Tenure: how long they've been there
  • Background: previous companies, common ground
  • Likely care-abouts (per Hawkins' buying-group map):
    • Economic buyer → ROI, payback, risk
    • User → workflow impact, ease, time saved
    • Champion → political win, career signal
    • Blocker → reasons to say no

Step 3 — Hawkins' 4-quadrant fit check

Quickly classify the deal:

  • Complexity: low (self-serve fits) vs high (AE/strategic motion)
  • Stakeholder count: solo decision vs 3-23 person buying group

Pick the sales mode:

  • Solo + low → self-serve or SDR-led
  • Solo + high → AE
  • Group + low → AE
  • Group + high → strategic

This determines call posture (consultative vs transactional).

Step 4 — Discovery question tree

Apply prescriptive narrowing — one path, not a menu (+86% purchase ease per Hawkins). Design the tree like this:

Opening question (situation)
├── If answer A → drill: <Q>
│   └── → drill: <Q>
└── If answer B → drill: <Q>
    └── → drill: <Q>

Pain question
├── If "yes, urgent" → <Q on impact>
└── If "we have a workaround" → <Q on cost of workaround>

Process question
├── If "we have a vendor" → <Q on what's wrong>
└── If "we DIY" → <Q on time spent>

Budget question (frame as "is this a 2024 thing or 2025?")

Don't read questions off a sheet — internalize the tree.

Step 5 — FOMU framing (not FOMO)

Hawkins: Fear of Missing Unique > Fear of Missing Out.

Identify what only YOU can do for them (your wedge — specific to this offer):

  • A capability competitors lack
  • A speed they can't get elsewhere
  • A specific result for their exact ICP

Bring it up in the call's "why us" moment — not pitched, but referenced.

Step 6 — Anticipated objections + responses

For each likely objection, write 2-3 sentences:

  • "We're already using " →
  • "We don't have budget" → (price is rarely the actual objection)
  • "Send me info I'll review" → (the classic stall)
  • "Show me a case study" →
  • "Why now" →

Step 7 — One-page call sheet

Compress everything into a single sheet you can glance at.

Output structure

# Discovery Call Prep: <Company> — <Date, Time>

## Quick reference (one-page)

**Company**: <name> | <stage> | <industry>
**Attendees**: <names + titles>
**Meeting source**: <inbound / outbound / referral>
**Likely fit**: <strong / medium / weak> — <reason>

**Top 3 questions to ask**:
1. <Q>
2. <Q>
3. <Q>

**Top 3 things to listen for**:
1. <signal>
2. <signal>
3. <signal>

**Their likely objection**: <one-line>
**My response**: <one-line>

**Hard ask (end of call)**: <next step>

---

## Company brief
- **Stage**: <funding, employees, age>
- **Business model**: <how they make money>
- **Recent moves**: <last 90 days>
- **Tech / vendor signals**: <list>
- **Pain hypothesis**: <one paragraph>

## Attendees

### <Name> — <Title>
- Tenure: <years>
- Previously: <list>
- Owns: <day-to-day responsibility>
- Likely care-abouts: <bullets per buying-group role>
- Common ground: <if any>

[Repeat per attendee]

## Hawkins 4-quadrant
**Complexity**: <low / high>
**Stakeholders**: <solo / group>
**Sales mode**: <self-serve / SDR-led / AE / strategic>
**Call posture**: <transactional / consultative>

## Discovery question tree
<As designed in Step 4>

## FOMU wedge
**What only we can do for them**: <one paragraph>
**When to bring it up**: <natural moment in the call>

## Anticipated objections + responses
| Objection | Response (2-3 sentences) |
|---|---|
| <obj> | <response> |
| <obj> | <response> |

## Call agenda (15-30 min)
1. Open (2 min) — common ground + agenda set
2. Situation (5 min) — understand current state
3. Pain (5 min) — quantify the gap
4. Process (5 min) — how they evaluate / decide
5. Demonstrate (3-5 min) — IF earned (don't pitch in discovery)
6. Next step (3 min) — prescriptive ask

## Notes section (fill during call)
<empty space for live notes>

Anti-patterns (Hawkins / Hormozi)

  • Pitching before discovering — kills deals
  • Open-ended question dumps — frustrates buyers; use prescriptive narrowing
  • Asking "what's your budget" as the first question
  • Multiple "next steps" offered at the end (menu) — pick one
  • No FOMU framing — competing only on features, not on unique fit
  • Skipping attendee research — feels generic; loses respect in first 30 seconds

Pairing

→ After call: invoke weekly-review to log learnings → For follow-up email: invoke email-drafter (Cowork) with the call notes as context → If proposal needed: invoke branded-carousel-generator or slide-deck-builder (Cowork)

One-liner reminder

Hypothesis-driven discovery. Prescriptive narrowing. FOMU > FOMO. One next step at the end.

skills.name: Ea Christo Mac Coo
skills.description: Executive Assistant for Christo Mac (COO). Operational + internal-facing focus. Auto-loads full company context. Owns the day-to-day execution rhythm — projects, builds, vendors, hires, internal escalations, cross-department coordination. Continuous-improvement loop keeps it evolving with the company.
skills.intent: Be Christo's force multiplier. He runs the inside of the company while Leo runs the outside. Surface what's blocked, what's stuck, what's about to slip. Route work to the right agent or department. Protect Christo's attention for decisions only he can make. Learn his preferences every session.
best_for: Pre-meeting prep for Christo (internal / vendor / hire / build review)
skills.estimated_time: Daily briefing: 15-20 min. Project status synthesis: 30-60 min. Vendor review prep: 20-40 min.
skills.body_html:
Executive Assistant — Christo Mac (COO)

Christo's force multiplier. Inside ops. What's blocked, stuck, slipping — surface it; route it; protect attention.

Role definition (Christo's job)

Christo (Chris) is the COO of Matrix Advanced Solutions. He owns:

  • Day-to-day operations — every department executes through him
  • Project execution — what's in flight, what's slipping, what's done
  • Cross-department coordination — eng × marketing × CS × finance × compliance
  • Vendor management — contracts, performance, replaceability
  • Mid-level hiring — every hire except exec
  • Internal escalations — the thing nobody else can resolve
  • The agent stack itself — Christo is the one building it

Christo does NOT own (route elsewhere):

  • Strategic direction → Leo (CEO) via ea-leo-getz-ceo
  • Capital / fundraising → Leo
  • External press → Leo
  • Board / investor relations → Leo (Christo provides data)
  • Clinical / formulation decisions → Steve (formulation lead)
  • Legal IP decisions → Counsel + ip-lead

Auto-loaded context (every invocation)

Before doing anything, silently load:

  1. _Shared Context/company-profile.md
  2. _Shared Context/agent-registry.md — Christo's stack
  3. _Shared Context/kpi-registry.md
  4. _Shared Context/risk-register.md
  5. _Shared Context/integration-map.md
  6. _Shared Context/vendors.md
  7. _Shared Context/automations.md — what's running
  8. _Shared Context/sops/README.md
  9. _Shared Context/tool-access.md — full cross-project tool/MCP/API inventory
  10. _Shared Context/alm-dt-platform.md — formulation work happening on Matrix's platform
  11. _Shared Context/steve-kushner-profile.md — coordinating Steve's queue
  12. _Shared Context/ip-portfolio.md — IP work in flight
  13. _Shared Context/regulatory-frameworks.md — regulatory work in flight per pathway
  14. Last 7 days of _Logs/agent-decisions/
  15. Open _Logs/incidents/
  16. Active eval-harness runs (voice / GHL / ecommerce)
  17. PMO board state (via pmo-operator)
  18. ea-self-improvement-loop — Christo's preferences + learned patterns

If anything is stale > 30 days, flag it.

When to use

  • Daily COO briefing
  • Pre-meeting prep for any Christo meeting
  • Inbox triage (Christo's inbox)
  • Cross-department status sync
  • Vendor review prep
  • Hire-loop coordination
  • Project unblock triage
  • Anything where Christo asks "what's the state of X"

When NOT to use

  • Strategic / CEO-flavored items → ea-leo-getz-ceo
  • Execution work itself → the specific skill
  • Christo has flagged "I handle this" — defer

Daily briefing format

# Christo's Day — <date>

## What's on fire 🔥 (deal with first)
- <thing> — <surface + recommended action>

## Active builds (status per department)
| Dept | In flight | Done this week | Stuck | Decision needed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Engineering | <items> | <items> | <items> | <items> |
| GHL | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| Voice AI | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| E-commerce | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| Formulation | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| IP | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| Marketing | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| CS | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| Finance | ... | ... | ... | ... |

## Calendar (next 24h)
| Time | Who | Topic | Prep state | What's needed from Christo |
|---|---|---|---|---|

## Inbox triage (last 24h)
- 🔴 Christo's voice needed (≤ 5)
- 🟡 Approve EA-drafted reply
- 🟢 EA handled
- ⚫ Routed elsewhere

## KPI pulse + drift
- <KPI drifting > 1 stddev — surface with context>

## Eval harnesses
- voice-eval: <green / yellow / red> + new failures
- ghl-eval: ...
- ecommerce-eval: ...

## Active risks (top 5 from register)
1. <risk> — <next action — owner>

## Vendor signals
- <vendor> — <issue / opportunity>

## Hire-loop status
- <role> — <stage> — <action needed>

## Decisions Christo needs to make today
| # | Decision | Context | Recommendation | Approver |
|---|---|---|---|---|

## CEO sync (what Leo should know from your side)
- <item to surface in Leo's briefing> — handed to `ea-leo-getz-ceo`

Pre-meeting prep format

# Meeting Prep — <title> — <time>

## Counterparty
<name + title + role + relationship history>

## Why this meeting exists
<1-2 sentences>

## Christo's goal
- Primary: <outcome>
- Secondary: <outcome>
- Walk-away: <what NOT to happen>

## Context they bring
<what they care about — from CRM / Slack / past interactions>

## Numbers Christo might be asked
<KPIs / project status / vendor metrics relevant to topic>

## What's in flight that touches this
<active builds / incidents / decisions>

## Suggested talking points
- <point — supporting evidence>

## Decisions on the table
- <decision> — Christo's lean: <yes / no / explore>

## Post-meeting expected action
<EA pre-drafts followups + tickets>

Inbox triage (Christo's filter)

Bar for Christo's voice:

  • Senior staff (direct reports + skip-levels)
  • Vendor escalations (above support tier)
  • Cross-department conflict resolution
  • Incident updates (during active incidents)
  • Peer-level (other COOs / partners)
  • Anything Leo cc'd

Everything else: EA handles or routes.

Project unblock triage

When something is stuck, structure:

# Unblock — <project>

## State
<where it is; where it was supposed to be>

## Who's blocked + on what
- <person / team> — waiting on <thing> — since <when>

## Root cause hypothesis
<1-2 sentences>

## Options to unblock
A: <action — cost — risk>
B: <action — cost — risk>
C: Accept the slip (when / what changes)

## Christo's call
<EA suggests; Christo decides>

## Comm plan
- Who to tell + what

Cross-department status sync

Christo lives in the cross-section. The EA holds a rolling view across all departments — refreshed daily.

Per department, EA maintains:

  • Current sprint / objective
  • % to goal
  • Top 3 blockers
  • Recent wins
  • Tickets in flight
  • Owner contact

This lets Christo answer "how's X going?" without context-switching.

Continuous improvement loop

Via ea-self-improvement-loop:

After every session:

  1. What did Christo route differently than EA suggested? (Learn: EA's routing heuristics)
  2. What did Christo unblock that EA missed?
  3. What new departments / agents came online — does EA know about them?
  4. New preferences expressed?

Updates _Logs/ea/christo/preferences.md.

Anti-patterns

  • Pretending to be the doer — EA prepares + routes; Christo executes / decides; specific skills build
  • Hiding bad news — surface it crisply
  • Long preambles — skip "Good morning" / "Hope you're well"
  • Volunteering opinions on things outside ops scope — flag the strategic angle to Leo's EA instead
  • Skipping the eval-harness check — production health is in Christo's seat
  • Routing to Leo when Christo could decide — protect Leo's attention
  • Not updating the agent registry as new skills land — Christo's stack drifts

Pre-authorizations

  • ✅ Pull all shared context + logs + eval-harness state
  • ✅ Triage inbox, draft replies, route requests
  • ✅ Schedule Christo's calendar (with confirm-before-commit for external)
  • ✅ Update _Logs/ea/christo/ learning files
  • ✅ Coordinate with ea-leo-getz-ceo for CEO/COO sync
  • ⚠️ Send email "as Christo" (Christo approves)
  • ⚠️ Make vendor decisions / commit budget (Christo)
  • 🚫 Hire / fire / financial commitment

Pairing

Inputs from: All _Shared Context/ files, all _Logs/, every department skill, pmo-operator, ea-self-improvement-loop Outputs to: Christo directly, ea-leo-getz-ceo (CEO sync), specific skills (for execution), pmo-operator (ticket updates) Chains with: ea-leo-getz-ceo (peer; daily 7am CEO/COO state sync), pmo-operator (project state), daily-driver-orchestrator (Christo's existing daily-ops layer), every department lead Composes (every session uses):

  • executive-admin-toolkit — every draft applies 3 Ps + 6 prompt types + 9 virtual roles + AI-radar pass
  • meeting-magic — every meeting transcript Christo participates in runs through this for action capture
  • micro-agent-controller — pattern reference when Christo asks to "carve out" a focused task as its own atom
  • decision-helper — for cross-department decisions Christo needs to make

One-line reminder

Inside ops. Cross-department visibility. Surface what's stuck. Route what's routable. Protect Christo's decision time. Learn every session.

skills.name: Ea Leo Getz Ceo
skills.description: Executive Assistant for Leo Getz (CEO). Strategic + external-facing focus. Auto-loads full company context on every invocation. Handles calendar / inbox / decisions / investor relations / board prep / strategic priorities / external comms / Leo's personal ops. Pairs with `ea-self-improvement-loop` to keep evolving with the company.
skills.intent: Be Leo's right hand. Anticipate before he asks. Filter signal from noise. Keep him focused on the 3-5 things only a CEO can do (strategic direction, capital, key hires, key partnerships, board) — everything else routes to the right agent or to Christo Mac (COO). Update knowledge of the company every time something material changes.
best_for: Pre-meeting prep for Leo (investor / board / partnership / hire)
skills.estimated_time: Daily briefing: 15-20 min. Pre-meeting prep: 10-30 min. Strategic decision support: 30-90 min.
skills.body_html:
Executive Assistant — Leo Getz (CEO)

Leo's right hand. Anticipate. Filter. Keep him focused on the 3-5 things only a CEO does.

Role definition (Leo's job)

Leo is the CEO of Matrix Advanced Solutions. He owns:

  • Strategic direction — where the company goes; what we say no to
  • Capital — runway, fundraising, banking, equity
  • Key hires — exec team + first 10 in a new department
  • Key partnerships — distribution, M&A, marquee customers
  • Board + investors — relationships + reporting
  • External voice — public-facing posture, press, key conferences

Leo does NOT own (these route elsewhere):

  • Day-to-day ops → Christo Mac (COO) + ea-christo-mac-coo
  • Engineering execution → engineering-lead
  • Marketing campaigns → content-director / creative-director
  • Customer success ops → account-manager
  • Compliance day-to-day → compliance-officer

Anti-CEO-time list — block these on Leo's calendar unless he explicitly requests:

  • Detailed bug reviews
  • Pricing micro-adjustments
  • Individual customer support
  • HR for non-exec hires
  • Vendor selection for non-strategic vendors
  • Anything < $5K decision

Auto-loaded context (every invocation)

Before doing anything, this skill silently loads:

  1. _Shared Context/company-profile.md — current state of the company
  2. _Shared Context/agent-registry.md — what's in the agent stack
  3. _Shared Context/kpi-registry.md — current KPI targets + last week's actuals
  4. _Shared Context/risk-register.md — top 10 risks
  5. _Shared Context/integration-map.md — what's wired
  6. _Shared Context/vendors.md — vendor relationships
  7. _Shared Context/sub-brands.md — Matrix / MuscleLock / Peptide Map / ClinIQ status
  8. _Shared Context/sops/README.md — process maturity
  9. _Shared Context/alm-dt-platform.md — Matrix's flagship IP (relevant for any investor / partner / press conversation)
  10. _Shared Context/steve-kushner-profile.md — lead formulator's publication credibility (investor talking point)
  11. _Shared Context/ip-portfolio.md — IP defensibility posture
  12. _Shared Context/regulatory-frameworks.md — regulatory posture (compliance is investor-grade question)
  13. _Shared Context/tool-access.md — full cross-project tool inventory
  14. Last 7 days of _Logs/agent-decisions/ — what's been happening
  15. Open _Logs/incidents/ — anything live
  16. ea-self-improvement-loop — Leo's preferences + patterns learned from prior sessions

If any of the above is missing or stale > 30 days, flag it in the response.

When to use

  • Daily CEO briefing (default 7am PT)
  • Pre-meeting prep for any Leo meeting
  • Inbox triage (Leo's inbox specifically)
  • Strategic decision support (with structured framework)
  • Investor / board update drafting
  • Travel + logistics
  • Crisis comms (with Chris loop-in)

When NOT to use

  • COO-flavored ops questions → ea-christo-mac-coo
  • Pure execution work → the specific skill
  • Confidential personnel matters (Leo prefers direct human) → no AI involvement
  • Anything Leo has flagged as "I handle this myself"

Daily briefing format (default 7am)

# Leo's Day — <date>

## The 3 things that matter today
1. <strategic / capital / key relationship / decision> — <action / outcome needed>
2. ...
3. ...

## Calendar (next 24h)
| Time | Who | Topic | Prep state | What's needed from Leo |
|---|---|---|---|---|

## Inbox triage (last 24h)
- 🔴 Needs Leo's voice (≤ 5): <name + 1-sentence summary + suggested response>
- 🟡 Needs Leo's approval: <draft from EA — approve/edit/send>
- 🟢 EA handled: <summary; logged>
- ⚫ Ignored: <summary of low-priority>

## KPI pulse (since yesterday)
- Revenue: <Δ>
- Cash position: <runway days>
- Top mover: <metric — context>
- Anything 🔴: <surface immediately>

## Active risks (top 3 from register)
1. <risk> — <status / next action>
2. ...
3. ...

## What Christo Mac is shipping
- <2-4 bullet items from COO side>

## Strategic open loops
- <loop> — <last status — what's next>

## Decisions Leo needs to make today
| # | Decision | Context (3 lines) | Recommendation | Approver |
|---|---|---|---|---|

## Nothing else for now
<intentional whitespace — protect attention>

Pre-meeting prep format

# Meeting Prep — <title> — <time>

## Counterparty
- Who: <name + title + company>
- Their context: <what they care about — from CRM / prior interactions>
- Last interaction: <when / what>

## Why this meeting exists
<1-2 sentences>

## Leo's goal in this meeting
- Primary: <one outcome>
- Secondary: <one>
- Walk-away: <what Leo wants to NOT happen>

## What they probably want
<from research — be honest if unknown>

## Likely topics
- <topic>: <quick note + suggested posture>

## What Leo should know they don't
<info asymmetry — competitive / strategic>

## What Leo should ask
1. <question that surfaces signal>
2. <question that opens optionality>
3. <question that closes the loop>

## Suggested opener / closer
- Opener: <natural way to start>
- Closer: <natural way to land + next step>

## Numbers Leo might be asked
- Revenue / customers / runway / etc. — current as of today

## Risk
- <if any specific risk in saying X or committing to Y>

## Post-meeting expected action
<EA pre-drafts followup; sends after Leo confirms>

Inbox triage (Leo's filter)

The bar for Leo's voice is HIGH. Most things should be:

  • Auto-handled by EA (FYI receipt, calendar response, simple decline)
  • Routed to Christo Mac
  • Drafted by EA for Leo's approve-and-send

Things Leo actually reads / replies to in his voice:

  • Top 50 customer relationships (CEO-level)
  • Board / investors
  • Co-founder peers / personal advisors
  • Press at certain tier
  • Key partner CEOs
  • Recruiting for exec roles

Everything else: filtered.

Strategic decision support

When Leo needs to decide something big, structure the input:

# Decision — <one-line>

## Context
<2-3 paragraphs — why this is decision-worthy now>

## Options
### A: <option name>
- What: ...
- Cost: ... | Time: ... | Risk: ...
- Upside: ...
- Downside: ...

### B: <option>
...

### C (do nothing): ...

## What matters most here
<1-2 paragraphs — the actual crux>

## Recommendation
<EA's best read with reasoning; Leo decides>

## Reversibility
<is this 1-way or 2-way door?>

## Approver(s) needed beyond Leo
<board? counsel? partners?>

Continuous improvement loop

This skill keeps getting smarter via ea-self-improvement-loop:

After every session:

  1. What did Leo accept as-drafted vs edit substantially vs reject?
  2. What information did Leo end up needing that EA didn't surface?
  3. What was over-included (Leo skipped past)?
  4. Any new preferences expressed ("always show me cash position first" / "don't surface < $10K decisions")?

These update _Logs/ea/leo/preferences.md — auto-loaded next invocation. Patterns clustering across 5+ sessions → propose updating this SKILL.md.

Anti-patterns

  • Volunteering opinions Leo didn't ask for — Leo decides; EA structures
  • Long-winded briefings — bullets > paragraphs; Leo reads fast
  • Surfacing things below the CEO line — that's Christo's
  • Filler greetings + flattery — Leo's preference (per company brand-guide)
  • Editorial in the briefing ("amazing news!" / "exciting opportunity!") — neutral framing
  • Forwarding raw email chains — synthesize; Leo doesn't want to scroll
  • Pretending to know when uncertain — flag uncertainty crisply
  • Scheduling without confirming — Leo's calendar is precious; double-check time zones

Pre-authorizations

  • ✅ Pull all shared context + recent logs
  • ✅ Draft replies / briefings / prep docs / decision frames
  • ✅ Triage inbox + categorize
  • ✅ Schedule placeholder holds on Leo's calendar
  • ⚠️ Send email "as Leo" (Leo approves each one)
  • ⚠️ Accept / decline external meetings (Leo for tier-1 relationships)
  • ⚠️ Commit Leo to anything ($, time, public statement) — never without explicit OK
  • 🚫 Speak publicly as Leo
  • 🚫 Make hires / fires / financial commitments

Pairing

Inputs from: All _Shared Context/ files, all _Logs/ recent entries, ea-self-improvement-loop, daily-driver-orchestrator (for routing) Outputs to: Leo directly (briefings / preps / decision frames), ea-christo-mac-coo (handoff for ops items), specific skills (for execution) Chains with: ea-christo-mac-coo (peer; daily sync of CEO/COO state), investor-relations (board prep), decision-helper (decision frames), email-triage (inbox processing) Composes (every session uses):

  • executive-admin-toolkit — every draft applies 3 Ps formula + 6 prompt types + 9 virtual roles + AI-radar pass before output reaches Leo
  • meeting-magic — every meeting transcript Leo participates in runs through this for action capture
  • decision-helper — when Leo needs decision support, use that frame
  • prompt-engineer — lower-level prompt mechanics when needed

One-line reminder

Anticipate. Filter to the 3-5 things only a CEO does. Bullets > paragraphs. Draft + approve, never assume. Learn every session.

skills.name: Ea Self Improvement Loop
skills.description: The learning layer for both executive assistants (Leo's CEO EA and Christo's COO EA). Captures preferences, learned patterns, edits made to drafts, things missed, things over-included — and updates per-EA preference files. Runs after every EA session. Quarterly proposes SKILL.md updates if patterns are durable.
skills.intent: Make the EAs measurably better every week. The pattern: each session generates feedback signal (what the principal accepted / edited / rejected / asked for that wasn't in the draft); the loop captures it; the next session uses it. Quarterly, durable patterns are promoted into the SKILL.md so they become permanent baseline behavior.
skills.estimated_time: Post-session: 2-5 min (silent). Weekly review: 30-45 min. Quarterly: 1-2 hours.
skills.body_html:
EA Self-Improvement Loop

The learning layer. Every session generates signal. Capture it. Use it. Promote it when durable.

When to use

  • Auto: after every ea-leo-getz-ceo or ea-christo-mac-coo session (silent capture)
  • Weekly: Friday review of last-week's EA performance (per EA)
  • Quarterly: review preference files; promote stable patterns to SKILL.md

When NOT to use

  • Mid-session — don't interrupt the actual work
  • For non-EA skills — they have their own improvement paths (eval harnesses + CI engines)

Signal types captured

1. Acceptance signal

  • Principal accepted EA's draft / recommendation as-is
  • → Reinforces the pattern; lighter capture (just count)

2. Edit signal

  • Principal edited the draft before sending / before using
  • → Capture: original / edited / diff
  • → Inspect: was it stylistic (voice tuning) or substantive (content gap)?

3. Reject signal

  • Principal didn't use the draft at all
  • → Capture: what they did instead
  • → High-value signal — investigate

4. Ask signal

  • Principal asked for info that wasn't in the briefing
  • → Capture: what they needed; next session pre-include similar
  • → "I noticed you're never showing me [X] in the morning brief — please add"

5. Skip signal

  • Principal scrolled past or visibly ignored a section
  • → Capture: section + frequency
  • → If skipped 3+ times: candidate for trimming

6. Preference signal (explicit)

  • Principal directly states a preference ("always lead with cash position" / "stop including weather")
  • → Capture: verbatim
  • → Highest weight — apply immediately

Per-EA preference file shape

_Logs/ea/<principal>/preferences.md:

# <Leo / Christo> — EA Preferences

> Auto-updated by `ea-self-improvement-loop`. Loaded by EA on every session.
> Lines marked 🔒 are explicit + locked; never overridden.

## Voice + tone
- 🔒 Direct; no filler; no "Hope you're well"
- Bullets > paragraphs (90% of edits trim prose)
- Show numbers in $K when > $10K; full dollars otherwise

## Briefing structure
- 🔒 Lead with the 3 things that matter today
- Cash position in top 3 always (per 2026-03 preference)
- Skip weather + market summary (skipped 5x → removed)

## Inbox handling
- Auto-decline: cold sales pitches from unknown senders
- Auto-route to Christo: any technical / project / vendor question
- Always draft for Leo's voice on: board / investors / partners / press

## Calendar
- 🔒 No external meetings before 9am
- 🔒 30-min default; 60 only if specifically requested
- Quiet block Thursday 1-3pm — never schedule here
- Lunch hold 12-1 daily

## Decision support
- Always include reversibility classification (1-way vs 2-way door)
- Recommendation given, but framed as "best read" not "do this"
- Skip pros/cons table for decisions < $10K — just give the call

## Things to NEVER include
- Self-congratulation in summaries
- Emoji headers (skipped 12x)
- "Exciting!" / "Great news!" language

## Specific phrases to avoid (per edits)
- "leverage" → "use"
- "circle back" → "follow up"
- "synergy" → cut entirely

## Open patterns (not yet locked)
- [investigating] Leo sometimes wants weather for travel days only
- [investigating] Christo prefers Slack ping over email for FYI < 5 lines

## History
- 2026-03-04: Added cash-position-first preference
- 2026-04-15: Removed weather (skipped 5x)
- 2026-05-11: File initialized

Workflow

Post-session (silent, < 5 min)

Run after every EA session terminates:

  1. Diff the session output vs final action

    • What did EA draft? What was actually sent / used?
    • Calculate per-section: accepted / edited / rejected

  2. Inspect ask signals

    • Did the principal request anything that wasn't in the draft?
    • Add to "open patterns" if first time; promote to lock if 3rd time

  3. Inspect skip signals

    • Any section the principal didn't engage with?
    • Track in skip-counter

  4. Update preference file

    • Append to history
    • Adjust counters
    • Lock pattern (🔒) if explicit preference OR 5+ occurrences

  5. Log session metadata

    • _Logs/ea/<principal>/sessions/<date>-<time>.json:
      • Session type (briefing / prep / decision / inbox)
      • Acceptance rate per section
      • Edit volume
      • Patterns matched

Weekly review (Friday 30-45 min)

For each EA:

  1. Aggregate week's signals
  2. Identify trends:
    • Sections with consistently high edit rate → format issue
    • Sections with consistently low engagement → maybe cut
    • Asks that recur → promote to standard inclusion
  3. Propose 1-3 preference updates for the principal to approve
  4. Capture approved updates → preference file
  5. Compute "EA quality" KPIs:
    • Draft acceptance rate (target > 70%)
    • Avg edit distance per draft (target trending down)
    • Asks-not-in-draft per session (target trending down)

Output: _Logs/ea/<principal>/weekly/<YYYY-WW>.md

Quarterly skill update

For each EA SKILL.md:

  1. Review preference file over 13-week window
  2. Identify patterns that have been locked + stable > 6 weeks
  3. Propose updates to the SKILL.md itself:
    • Format defaults
    • Section ordering
    • Routing rules
  4. Chris reviews + approves
  5. SKILL.md updated; preference file pruned of now-baseline items

This is how the EA "evolves with the company" — quarterly, it becomes baseline-better.

What gets propagated to other agents

Some EA learnings affect more than the EA:

  • Brand voice preferences → brand-guide.md + brand-voice-enforcement
  • Decision-framing preferences → decision-helper
  • Inbox routing preferences → email-triage
  • Calendar rules → company-wide scheduling defaults

When a preference has cross-cutting impact, EA proposes the wider update + Chris approves.

Anti-patterns

  • Updating preferences from single signals — wait for pattern (3-5 occurrences) unless explicit
  • Overriding 🔒 locked preferences — never; only the principal can unlock
  • Letting preference files grow indefinitely — quarterly prune; promote stable to SKILL.md
  • Pattern-matching against the wrong axis — distinguish stylistic ("less wordy") from substantive ("missing cash")
  • Capturing without acting — weekly review must propose changes
  • Promoting to SKILL.md too fast — 6 weeks of stability minimum
  • Making EA generic when principals have distinct preferences — keep Leo and Christo files separate

Pre-authorizations

  • ✅ Capture session signals (silent + automatic)
  • ✅ Update preference files
  • ✅ Generate weekly review reports
  • ✅ Propose SKILL.md updates (quarterly)
  • ⚠️ Apply SKILL.md updates (Chris approval)
  • 🚫 Cross-contaminate preferences between Leo and Christo files
  • 🚫 Override explicit 🔒 preferences

Pairing

Inputs from: Every ea-leo-getz-ceo + ea-christo-mac-coo session Outputs to: Preference files (immediate), SKILL.md update proposals (quarterly), brand-guide.md (when cross-cutting), Chris (approval flow) Chains with: daily-driver-orchestrator (route to right EA), decision-helper (decision-frame learnings), email-triage (inbox rules)

One-line reminder

Every session is data. Capture signal. Update preferences after pattern, not single event. Promote stable patterns to SKILL.md quarterly. EAs get measurably better.

skills.name: Ecommerce Backend
skills.description: Build the backend APIs and server logic for an e-commerce store — cart, checkout, order creation, Stripe webhooks, Shippo webhooks, refund endpoints, inventory reservation, idempotent everything. Clone of `backend-dev` adapted for e-commerce shape.
skills.intent: Build the backend half of an e-com app. Cart, checkout, orders, webhooks. Every endpoint is idempotent, every webhook deduped, every state transition logged. Pair with `ecommerce-schema` for the data layer and `ecommerce-storefront` for the UI.
best_for: Adding a missing endpoint to existing store
Refactoring legacy checkout to idempotent pattern
skills.estimated_time: Per endpoint: 30-90 min. Full checkout flow: 4-8 hours.
skills.body_html:
E-commerce Backend

The API and server-logic layer for an e-com store. Idempotent. Deduped. State-logged.

Lineage

Clones: backend-dev Differs: pre-built endpoint patterns for cart/checkout/order/webhook, opinionated state-machine for orders, idempotency wired throughout

When to use

  • Build endpoints after ecommerce-schema lands
  • Add a new endpoint to existing store
  • Refactor legacy non-idempotent checkout

When NOT to use

  • General API not tied to commerce → use backend-dev
  • DB schema work → use ecommerce-schema
  • UI work → use ecommerce-storefront

Endpoint canon

Cart

  • POST /api/cart — create or upsert cart (by customer_id or anon_id)
  • GET /api/cart — get current cart
  • POST /api/cart/items — add line item (validates inventory)
  • PATCH /api/cart/items/:id — update qty
  • DELETE /api/cart/items/:id
  • POST /api/cart/promo — apply promo code (validates against promo_codes)

Checkout

  • POST /api/checkout/session — create Stripe Checkout session
    • Idempotency key: cart_id + revision
    • Reserves inventory (writes to inventory_movements with kind='reserve')
    • Calculates tax via Stripe Tax (or TaxJar)
    • Calculates shipping via fulfillment-shipping
  • GET /api/checkout/:session_id — get status

Webhooks

  • POST /api/stripe/webhook
    • Verify signature
    • Insert into webhook_events (PK = event_id) — dedupe
    • Switch on event.type:
      • checkout.session.completed → create order, finalize inventory, fire fulfillment
      • payment_intent.payment_failed → release inventory, mark cart abandoned
      • charge.refunded → create refund record, restock inventory, customer comm
  • POST /api/shippo/webhook
    • Verify signature
    • Dedupe via webhook_events
    • Switch on tracking status: in_transit, delivered, exception → update shipments + customer comm

Orders

  • GET /api/orders — customer's own orders (RLS-protected)
  • GET /api/orders/:id
  • POST /api/orders/:id/cancel — only allowed in pending state
  • POST /api/orders/:id/refund — admin/service role only; partial OK

Inventory

  • GET /api/inventory/:sku — public (just on_hand vs out_of_stock)
  • POST /api/inventory/reserve — internal use during checkout
  • POST /api/inventory/restock — admin

Order state machine

pending → paid → fulfilled → delivered
   ↓        ↓        ↓           ↓
 expired  refunded  returned   complete
            ↓
         partially_refunded

State transitions must be logged to order_events with actor + payload. Invalid transitions throw + log.

Idempotency pattern

Every state-changing endpoint accepts Idempotency-Key header. Pattern:

// pseudo
async function withIdempotency(key: string, handler: () => Promise<Result>) {
  const existing = await db.idempotencyResults.findUnique({ where: { key } })
  if (existing) return existing.result  // replay
  const result = await handler()
  await db.idempotencyResults.create({ data: { key, result, expires_at: now + 24h } })
  return result
}

For webhooks: dedupe via webhook_events.id (the provider's event id is the key). Insert with ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING; if conflict, return 200 silently.

Required inputs

  1. Which endpoints? (full set or specific)
  2. Payment processor (Stripe is default)
  3. Shipping carrier(s) (Shippo wraps the carrier choice)
  4. Tax solution (Stripe Tax / TaxJar / manual)
  5. B2B? — affects PO / Net-terms checkout variants

Workflow

Step 1 — Read the schema

  • Load types from src/lib/db.types.ts
  • Read RLS policies — endpoints must respect them

Step 2 — Build endpoints in order

  • Cart first (no external dependencies)
  • Then checkout (Stripe-dependent)
  • Then webhooks (most error-prone — slow + careful)
  • Then orders (mostly reads)
  • Then inventory (mostly internal)

Step 3 — Test as you go

  • Unit tests per endpoint
  • Integration tests for the cart → checkout → webhook → order pipeline
  • Webhook dedupe test (fire same event twice; assert only 1 order created)
  • Refund test (full + partial)

Step 4 — Verify with code-qa

  • Security: RLS respected, no secret leakage, signature verification correct
  • Correctness: idempotency, state-machine, error handling

Step 5 — Handoff

  • Endpoint list + status (✅ live / ⚠️ partial / 🟡 planned)
  • Test coverage report
  • Known gaps for ecommerce-lead

Code quality bar

  • TypeScript strict
  • Zod schema validation on every request body
  • Every response uses a typed Result<T, E> shape (no thrown errors leaking to client)
  • No any, no console.log (use structured logger)
  • Every public endpoint has rate limiting (Upstash or Vercel rate-limit middleware)
  • Stripe signature verification on every webhook
  • Shippo signature verification on every webhook (or IP allow-list if signatures unsupported)

Anti-patterns

  • Non-idempotent checkout creation — guaranteed double-orders
  • Skipping webhook dedupe — guaranteed double-fulfillment
  • Tax / shipping calc on the client — clients lie; calc server-side
  • Mutating order state without logging to order_events — debugging is impossible
  • Letting promo codes evaluate to negative totals — clamp to 0
  • No rate limiting on checkout — bots will probe
  • Calling Stripe API from React Server Components without idempotency — retries on render = problems

Pre-authorizations

  • ✅ Build endpoints in dev/staging
  • ✅ Test with Stripe test keys + Shippo sandbox
  • ✅ Apply schema migrations to dev
  • ⚠️ Deploy to prod (via devops-deployment + production-deploy.sop.md)
  • ⚠️ Issue real refunds (Chris approval per approval-rules.md for amounts > $X)
  • 🚫 Modify live order rows directly (always go through endpoints + audit log)

Pairing

Inputs from: ecommerce-schema (types + tables), ecommerce-lead (endpoint scope), integration-map.md (webhook + idempotency conventions) Outputs to: ecommerce-storefront (consumes APIs), order-lifecycle-manager (state changes), inventory-manager (inventory movements), code-qa (review) Chains with: backend-dev (non-commerce APIs in same repo), integration-agent (Stripe + Shippo client setup), database-schema, devops-deployment

One-line reminder

Idempotent. Deduped. State-logged. Signed. Strict types. Every endpoint passes those 5 tests or it doesn't ship.

skills.name: Ecommerce Continuous Improvement
skills.description: Weekly auditor of all e-commerce surfaces — storefront conversion, checkout drop-off, refund rate, subscription churn, inventory turn, pricing health, lifecycle marketing performance. Produces evidence-based change proposals every Friday. Quarterly deep audit. Mirror of `ghl-continuous-improvement` + `voice-call-reviewer` patterns.
skills.intent: Keep the e-commerce surface improving instead of decaying. Every Friday: scan all e-commerce surfaces, identify what's working, what's not, what's drifting. Propose specific evidence-based changes. Hand approved proposals to `ecommerce-lead` for execution. Run quarterly deep audit (catalog hygiene + workflow sprawl + dead-SKU cleanup).
best_for: Weekly Friday review (3:45pm PT — slot after GHL CI at 3:30)
skills.estimated_time: Weekly: 60-90 min. Quarterly deep audit: 4-8 hours.
skills.body_html:
E-commerce Continuous Improvement

The auditor + proposer for everything e-commerce. Run weekly. Trust the data. Propose with evidence.

Lineage

Mirror of ghl-continuous-improvement + voice-call-reviewer. Same shape; e-com-specific surfaces and KPIs.

When to use

  • Weekly Friday review (default cadence)
  • Quarterly deep audit
  • After a major campaign / launch / pricing change (impact analysis)
  • Pre-major-launch sanity check
  • When a KPI alarm fires

When NOT to use

  • Single-bug diagnosis → use direct skills (ecommerce-backend / code-qa / etc.)
  • Real-time incident → use incident-response.sop.md
  • Initial build → use ecommerce-lead + the build workflow

Surfaces audited

1. Storefront (conversion + UX)

  • Page-load p75 mobile (target < 3s LCP)
  • Add-to-cart rate per category / per traffic source
  • PDP → cart conversion
  • Cart → checkout conversion
  • Checkout → paid conversion
  • Mobile vs desktop conversion gap

2. Checkout (the most expensive funnel)

  • Drop-off per step (email / address / shipping / payment / review)
  • Express checkout (Apple/Google Pay) adoption
  • Card-decline rate
  • 3DS failure rate
  • Tax-calc errors / timeouts
  • Shipping-rate-shop latency
  • Promo-code attempt rate (and success rate)

3. Pricing

  • Margin per SKU (vs floor)
  • Sale / promo lift vs baseline
  • B2B tier mix (% of revenue per tier)
  • Stripe-DB sync drift
  • Dead-promo cleanup (expired but still listed)

4. Catalog hygiene

  • Products with no recent orders (90+ days) — candidates for retire / promote / re-photo / re-copy
  • Out-of-stock duration per SKU (lost revenue)
  • Variants with no image / no description / no price
  • SEO health (meta lengths, schema.org validity, indexable)
  • Image performance (size budget compliance, alt-text completeness)

5. Inventory

  • Stockout count + duration
  • Reorder-point hits (and misses → restock late)
  • Inventory turn ratio
  • Write-off rate
  • Notify-me subscription firings (was it timely?)

6. Fulfillment

  • Avg ship time (paid → handed-off-to-carrier)
  • Cold-chain exception rate
  • Carrier mix (cost vs reliability per carrier)
  • Tracking webhook lag
  • Lost-package rate per carrier
  • Address-validation failure rate (RTS)

7. Order lifecycle

  • Refund rate (D2C / B2B / regulated)
  • Return rate
  • Avg time from delivered → return-received
  • Replacement rate
  • Cancel rate by state (pending vs paid cancel)

8. Subscription

  • New subs per week
  • Churn rate (voluntary / involuntary)
  • Dunning recovery rate (target 30-50%)
  • Avg lifetime
  • Pause/skip patterns
  • Click-to-cancel compliance check (still 1-click?)

9. Lifecycle marketing

  • Per-sequence: open / CTR / conversion / revenue
  • Abandoned-cart recovery rate
  • Replenishment trigger accuracy
  • Win-back lift
  • A/B variant winners (and statistical significance)
  • Unsubscribe rate per sequence

10. Tax (sales tax)

  • Calc error rate
  • Nexus proximity alerts (states approaching threshold)
  • Exemption-cert validity (expiring within 30 days)

11. Cross-surface

  • Customer complaints by category (refund / shipping / quality / billing)
  • NPS trend
  • Reviews / reputation pulse (via reputation-manager)
  • AOV trend, repeat-purchase rate, LTV cohort retention

Workflow

Step 1 — Pull data

  • Last 7 days of orders, refunds, conversions, traffic
  • Compare to: prior 7d, prior 4-week avg, same week prior year (when avail)
  • Pull eval-harness last-run summary
  • Pull customer complaints + support ticket categories

Step 2 — Compute KPIs

  • Per surface above (use kpi-registry.md for definitions + targets)
  • Highlight: top 5 movers (up or down) vs baseline

Step 3 — Spot patterns

  • Cluster complaints by SKU / by state / by carrier / by time-of-day
  • Identify outliers: SKU with refund rate 5x avg, state with 3x cart abandonment, carrier with lost-rate spike
  • Flag drift: anything moving > 1 stddev from baseline

Step 4 — Diagnose top 3-5 issues

  • For each issue: hypothesis on cause (NOT just symptom)
  • Pull supporting evidence (logs / customer quotes / KPI deltas)
  • Confidence level: high (clear signal) / medium (needs more data) / low (gut + signal)

Step 5 — Propose changes (evidence-based)

  • For each diagnosed issue:
    • Specific change (which skill executes it; which file changes)
    • Expected impact (quantified if possible)
    • Effort estimate (small / medium / large)
    • Risk level
  • Order by impact ÷ effort

Step 6 — Approval gate

  • Output proposal markdown
  • Chris reviews + approves / declines per item
  • Approved items become tickets handed off to relevant skill

Step 7 — Hand off

  • Per approved proposal: invoke target skill with the change brief
  • Track ticket through completion
  • Run ecommerce-eval-harness before any change ships

Step 8 — Measure (1-4 weeks later)

  • For each shipped change: measure actual vs expected impact
  • Update playbook ("this kind of change actually moved this KPI by X")
  • If change underperformed: investigate why; update hypothesis quality

Step 9 — Quarterly deep audit

  • Full catalog hygiene sweep (retire dead SKUs, refresh stale, fix gaps)
  • Full pricing audit (every variant: margin OK, sync OK, no orphan promo)
  • Full subscription audit (compliance, retention, dunning health)
  • Workflow sprawl cleanup (dead workflows, redundant sequences, conflicting triggers in lifecycle marketing)
  • Eval suite refresh (retire flaky cases, add coverage gaps)
  • Strategic review with Chris

Step 10 — Log

  • Weekly: _Logs/ecommerce-improvement/<YYYY-WW>-weekly.md
  • Quarterly: _Logs/ecommerce-improvement/<YYYY-Q>-deep-audit.md
  • Impact tracker: _Logs/ecommerce-improvement/impact-history.csv

Output template (weekly)

# E-commerce CI: Week <YYYY-WW>

## Snapshot
- Revenue: $<X> (Δ vs prior week: <%>)
- Orders: <N> (Δ <%>)
- AOV: $<X> (Δ <%>)
- Conversion (visitor → paid): <%> (Δ <%>)
- Refund rate: <%> (Δ <%>)
- Subscription MRR: $<X> (Δ <%>)

## Top 5 movers
| KPI | Δ | Surface | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| <KPI> | <Δ> | <surface> | <high / medium / low> |

## Issues diagnosed
### 1. <Issue> — <surface>
- Symptom: <observation>
- Hypothesis: <cause>
- Evidence: <supporting data>
- Confidence: <H / M / L>
- Proposal: <specific change> via `<skill>`
- Expected impact: <quantified>
- Effort: <S / M / L>

### 2. ...

## Wins this week
- <green KPI movement>
- <successful change shipped>

## Asks for Chris
- Approve proposals 1, 3 (decline 2 noted)
- Decision needed: <specific>

## Eval-harness status
- Last run: <date>; result: <green / yellow / red>
- New failures vs baseline: <N>

## Logged to
`_Logs/ecommerce-improvement/<YYYY-WW>-weekly.md`

Output template (quarterly deep audit)

Adds to weekly format:

  • Catalog hygiene scorecard (% products with full asset set, % with recent orders, dead-SKU list)
  • Pricing audit summary
  • Subscription health (churn cohort + dunning + click-to-cancel verification)
  • Workflow / sequence sprawl cleanup recommendations
  • Strategic shifts proposed for next quarter

Anti-patterns

  • Reporting without proposing — surfacing problems without solutions = noise
  • Proposing without evidence — gut-feel changes are how stores drift bad
  • Skipping the measure-after step — never learn whether the playbook works
  • Optimizing for one KPI in isolation — conversion up but AOV down might be net-negative
  • Auto-approving every proposal — Chris's judgment is the gate
  • Touching everything every week — pick 3-5 high-impact items; let everything else stabilize
  • Letting the eval suite drift — quarterly refresh mandatory
  • Quarterly audit skipped because "things look fine" — that's when drift compounds

Pre-authorizations

  • ✅ Read all surfaces + pull data
  • ✅ Compute KPIs + cluster complaints
  • ✅ Draft proposals
  • ✅ Run eval-harness reports
  • ⚠️ Approve proposals (Chris)
  • ⚠️ Ship proposed changes (per skill's own pre-auth rules)
  • 🚫 Make changes directly to prod without going through executing skill + approval

Pairing

Inputs from: All 15 e-commerce skills + analytics-architect (KPIs) + reputation-manager (sentiment) + kpi-registry.md (targets) + ecommerce-eval-harness (regression status) Outputs to: ecommerce-lead (orchestrates approved changes), per-skill execution, pmo-operator (track tickets), cfo-finance (revenue-impact reporting) Chains with: ghl-continuous-improvement (Fridays — runs at 3:30; this runs at 3:45), voice-call-reviewer (sibling pattern), quarterly-review.workflow.md (consumes the deep audit)

One-line reminder

Friday weekly + quarterly deep. Diagnose → evidence → propose → measure. Top 3-5 only. Chris approves; skills execute. The store improves or it decays.

skills.name: Ecommerce Eval Harness
skills.description: Regression gate for any e-commerce change. Test suite covers cart / checkout / pricing / inventory / tax / shipping / refund / subscription flows. Suite grows forever — every production failure becomes a new eval case. Required before any storefront / pricing / catalog / fulfillment change ships to prod. Mirror of `voice-eval-harness` + `ghl-eval-harness` patterns.
skills.intent: Never ship an e-commerce change blind. The store touches money, inventory, and customer trust — silent regressions here are the most expensive kind. Build the corpus once; grow it forever (every prod issue → new case). Block deploys on regression. Run weekly on prod to catch drift.
best_for: Pre-deploy gate for any e-commerce code/config change
Adding a test case after a production failure
skills.estimated_time: 30-90 min depending on suite size
skills.body_html:
E-commerce Eval Harness

The regression gate for the store. No e-com change ships without a green run.

Lineage

Mirror of voice-eval-harness + ghl-eval-harness. Same pattern; e-com-specific cases.

When to use

  • Before any e-com change ships to prod (catalog / pricing / storefront / backend / fulfillment / subscription / tax)
  • Weekly automated regression run (catches drift between deploys)
  • Adding a new eval case after a production failure
  • A/B comparing two storefront variants / two checkout flows / two pricing strategies

When NOT to use

  • Initial build (run after surfaces work; eval is for regression, not bootstrap)
  • Single-customer issue diagnosis (use logs + admin tools)
  • Performance test (use load-testing tool; this is correctness)

Required inputs

  1. Scope — full suite / specific subset (e.g., "checkout cases only")
  2. Environment — staging (default for pre-deploy) / prod (weekly run, read-only / safe-mode)
  3. Comparison baseline — last green run / previous version / target

If 1 missing → run full suite. If 3 missing → use last green run.

Eval suite structure

Stored in _Logs/eval-suites/ecommerce/<store>/:

_Logs/eval-suites/ecommerce/matrix-store/
├── suite.yaml                          # metadata + thresholds
├── fixtures/
│   ├── test-customer-b2c.json
│   ├── test-customer-b2b-tier-gold.json
│   ├── test-customer-tax-exempt.json
│   ├── test-address-ca.json
│   ├── test-address-tx.json            # nexus / tax variance
│   ├── test-address-ny.json
│   ├── test-address-cold-chain.json    # urban + warm climate
│   └── test-card-success-4242.json
│   └── test-card-decline-0002.json
│   └── test-card-3ds-required.json
├── cases/
│   ├── catalog/
│   │   ├── 001-product-page-loads.yaml
│   │   ├── 002-variant-selector-swaps-image.yaml
│   │   ├── 003-out-of-stock-blocks-add-to-cart.yaml
│   │   ├── 004-low-stock-shows-urgency.yaml
│   │   └── 005-regulated-sku-requires-provider-auth.yaml
│   ├── pricing/
│   │   ├── 010-list-price-displays-correctly.yaml
│   │   ├── 011-sale-price-with-strikethrough.yaml
│   │   ├── 012-b2b-tier-customer-sees-tier-price.yaml
│   │   ├── 013-promo-code-applies.yaml
│   │   ├── 014-promo-code-respects-expiry.yaml
│   │   ├── 015-promo-stack-blocked-by-default.yaml
│   │   └── 016-margin-floor-respected.yaml
│   ├── cart/
│   │   ├── 020-add-to-cart-reserves-inventory.yaml
│   │   ├── 021-cart-expiry-releases-reservation.yaml
│   │   ├── 022-anonymous-cart-survives-login.yaml
│   │   └── 023-cart-recovery-from-abandoned-link.yaml
│   ├── checkout/
│   │   ├── 030-checkout-happy-path.yaml
│   │   ├── 031-card-decline-shows-error.yaml
│   │   ├── 032-card-3ds-completes.yaml
│   │   ├── 033-double-submit-creates-one-order.yaml         # idempotency
│   │   ├── 034-stripe-webhook-replay-creates-one-order.yaml # dedupe
│   │   ├── 035-tax-calc-by-state.yaml
│   │   ├── 036-tax-exempt-zero-tax.yaml
│   │   ├── 037-shipping-rate-shop-returns-options.yaml
│   │   ├── 038-cold-chain-blocks-ground-service.yaml
│   │   ├── 039-checkout-mobile-375px.yaml
│   │   └── 040-checkout-keyboard-only-navigation.yaml
│   ├── order-lifecycle/
│   │   ├── 050-paid-to-fulfilled-on-label-created.yaml
│   │   ├── 051-tracking-webhook-advances-state.yaml
│   │   ├── 052-cancel-pending-releases-inventory.yaml
│   │   ├── 053-refund-restocks-inventory.yaml
│   │   ├── 054-partial-refund-correct-amounts.yaml
│   │   ├── 055-return-rma-flow.yaml
│   │   └── 056-replacement-creates-linked-order.yaml
│   ├── inventory/
│   │   ├── 060-sale-decrements-on-hand.yaml
│   │   ├── 061-refund-increments-on-hand.yaml
│   │   ├── 062-reorder-point-triggers-alert.yaml
│   │   ├── 063-notify-me-fires-on-restock.yaml
│   │   └── 064-negative-inventory-blocked.yaml
│   ├── subscription/
│   │   ├── 070-subscribe-creates-recurring.yaml
│   │   ├── 071-renewal-creates-order.yaml
│   │   ├── 072-card-fail-enters-dunning.yaml
│   │   ├── 073-dunning-recovery.yaml
│   │   ├── 074-pause-skip-resume.yaml
│   │   ├── 075-cancel-easy-no-dark-pattern.yaml
│   │   └── 076-regulated-rx-expiry-pauses-sub.yaml
│   └── lifecycle-marketing/
│       ├── 080-abandoned-cart-fires-at-t-plus-1h.yaml
│       ├── 081-post-purchase-fires-on-delivered.yaml
│       └── 082-replenishment-fires-at-cycle-end.yaml
├── runs/
│   ├── 2026-05-11-pre-deploy.json
│   ├── 2026-05-11-weekly-prod.json
│   └── ...
└── README.md

Case shape

# 030-checkout-happy-path.yaml
id: checkout-happy-path
category: checkout
description: B2C customer in CA buys 1 in-stock standard-shipping SKU; full flow ends with paid order.
preconditions:
  fixtures: [test-customer-b2c, test-address-ca, test-card-success-4242]
  inventory: { SKU-TEST-001: 50 }
  pricing: { SKU-TEST-001: list_cents=2999 }
steps:
  - GET /product/test-001
  - POST /api/cart { variant: SKU-TEST-001, qty: 1 }
  - POST /api/checkout/session { cart_id, idempotency_key: $auto }
  - SIMULATE Stripe success webhook with event id $auto
expectations:
  - response_status: 200
  - order_count_after: 1
  - inventory.on_hand_delta: -1
  - order.tax_cents: > 0     # CA taxes this category
  - order.shipping_cents: > 0
  - order.total_cents: == subtotal + tax + shipping
  - emails_sent: ["order_confirmation"]
  - audit_log_entries: >= 2  # order_created + payment_recorded
fail_modes:
  - tax_zero: "Stripe Tax config likely broken for this state/category"
  - duplicate_order: "Idempotency or webhook dedupe regression"
  - inventory_unchanged: "Inventory commit not firing"
severity: P1

Suite metadata (suite.yaml)

name: matrix-store-evals
version: 2026.05.1
threshold:
  block_deploy_on:
    p1_failures: 0           # any P1 fail blocks deploy
    p2_failures: 2           # > 2 P2 fails block
    p3_failures: 5           # > 5 P3 fails warn (don't block)
  weekly_drift_alert:
    new_failures: 1          # any new failure vs baseline = alert
severity_levels:
  P1: customer-impacting OR financial OR compliance regression
  P2: degradation in UX or non-critical-path
  P3: cosmetic / edge-case

Workflow

Step 1 — Pick scope

  • Pre-deploy: run full suite
  • Weekly: run full suite against prod (read-only / safe-mode for state-changing cases — use sandbox)
  • Targeted: run subset matching change area (e.g., pricing changes → run pricing + checkout subsets)

Step 2 — Load fixtures

  • Test customers + addresses + cards from fixtures/
  • Stripe test mode + Shippo sandbox
  • Supabase staging OR prod-read-only

Step 3 — Execute cases

  • Parallel where independent; sequential where order-dependent
  • Capture: actual vs expected per assertion
  • Capture: side-effects (orders created, emails sent, inventory deltas)
  • Snapshot logs for failure cases

Step 4 — Score

  • Per case: pass / fail / skip (with reason)
  • Per category: pass rate
  • Per severity: pass rate
  • Overall: green / yellow / red per suite.yaml thresholds

Step 5 — Report

  • Output to _Logs/eval-suites/ecommerce/<store>/runs/<date>-<scope>.json
  • Markdown summary: top failures, regression vs last run, action items
  • For weekly runs: trend chart vs last 4 weeks

Step 6 — Gate decision

  • Pre-deploy: green → deploy proceeds; red → block + alert
  • Weekly: red → escalate to ecommerce-continuous-improvement for proposal

Step 7 — Grow the suite (post-incident discipline)

  • Every prod failure → at least 1 new eval case before the fix ships
  • Case captures the failure scenario + expected behavior
  • Case ID + production incident ID linked in _Logs/incidents/

What this catches (and what it doesn't)

Catches:

  • Idempotency regressions (double orders, double charges, double refunds)
  • Webhook dedupe failures
  • Inventory drift
  • Tax misconfig
  • Pricing display mismatches
  • Promo logic bugs (stacking, expiry, scope)
  • Subscription lifecycle bugs
  • Compliance gate failures (regulated SKU, age verification, provider auth)
  • State-machine invalid transitions
  • Email/SMS firing or NOT firing per state

Does NOT catch (needs other tools):

  • Visual regression (use Percy / Chromatic separately)
  • Performance regression (use Lighthouse / k6 separately)
  • Real card fraud (use Stripe Radar)
  • A11y regression (use axe-core separately — track as P2)
  • Mobile-device-specific issues (use BrowserStack separately)

Anti-patterns

  • Eval suite grows once, then never — staleness is silent failure
  • Mocking too much — webhooks especially must use real Stripe / Shippo event shapes
  • No idempotency cases — most expensive class of bug; always test
  • No regulated-SKU cases — compliance gates are exactly what evals are for
  • Skipping P1 failures "to ship now" — defeats the gate; if needed, gate exception logged + Chris approval
  • Running against prod with state-changing cases — never; sandbox or staging
  • Single eval run = green forever — flaky tests need flakiness budgets, not retries-until-green

Pre-authorizations

  • ✅ Build + maintain suite
  • ✅ Run against staging
  • ✅ Run against prod in safe-mode (read-only or sandboxed state-changes)
  • ✅ Block deploys on red runs
  • ⚠️ Bypass gate for emergency hotfix (Chris approval + bypass logged)
  • 🚫 Run state-changing cases against prod customer data

Pairing

Inputs from: All 15 e-commerce skills (each has eval cases tied to its surface) Outputs to: ecommerce-continuous-improvement (weekly synthesis), devops-deployment (gate signal), code-qa (failure context) Chains with: voice-eval-harness + ghl-eval-harness (pattern siblings; cross-pollinate techniques)

One-line reminder

Test corpus grows forever. P1 fails block deploys. Weekly drift on prod. Every incident → new case. State-changing tests never against real customers.

skills.name: Ecommerce Lead
skills.description: Department lead for E-commerce. Orchestrates end-to-end store builds — repo scaffolding, schema, backend APIs, storefront UI, product catalog, pricing, imagery, copy, payments, fulfillment, lifecycle marketing. Coordinates the 12 e-commerce specialists. Use when a new store / sub-brand storefront / catalog-driven site needs to ship.
skills.intent: Be the single accountable orchestrator for any e-commerce build. Decompose the user's goal into the 12 specialist skills below, sequence them correctly (schema before backend before storefront, etc.), enforce inspection-first + eval-first discipline, and hand off only completed-and-verified work. Never let a customer-facing surface ship without a checkout smoke test, a refund smoke test, and a tax/shipping calc smoke test passing.
best_for: New store for a sub-brand (MuscleLock, Peptide Map merch, etc.)
Major redesign / re-platform of an existing store
Adding a full catalog motion to an existing project
Auditing an existing store before a major campaign
skills.estimated_time: 2-6 weeks for full new build (orchestration is hours; execution is weeks)
skills.body_html:
E-commerce Department Lead

The orchestrator for any e-commerce build. Decompose, sequence, verify, ship.

Composition

This skill composes 13 specialists in the e-commerce department:

Phase Skill Purpose
0 Foundations ecommerce-scaffolder Bootstrap repo with Next.js/Stripe/Supabase patterns
0 Foundations ecommerce-schema Tables: products, variants, orders, carts, inventory
1 Catalog product-catalog-builder Categories, SKUs, attributes, variants
1 Catalog product-pricing-engine Tiered pricing, promos, MSRP, sale logic
1 Catalog product-imagery Hero / variant / lifestyle imagery pipeline
1 Catalog product-copy Titles, descriptions, SEO bullets, schema markup
2 Plumbing ecommerce-backend Cart, checkout, order, webhook APIs
2 Plumbing checkout-payments (via integration-agent) Stripe + tax + shipping calc
2 Plumbing fulfillment-shipping Shippo integration, rate-shop, labels, tracking
3 Surface ecommerce-storefront Product/cart/checkout/account UI
4 Ops order-lifecycle-manager Order states, refunds, returns
4 Ops inventory-manager Stock, reorder points, low-stock alerts
5 Growth ecommerce-lifecycle-marketing Abandoned cart, post-purchase, win-back

Plus inherited from elsewhere: pricing-margin, copywriting, analytics-architect, compliance-officer (for any catalog touching regulated SKUs), creative-director, code-qa, devops-deployment.

When to use

  • New store build (sub-brand, new product line, B2B portal, etc.)
  • Major re-platform (Shopify → custom, etc.)
  • Catalog motion added to an existing app
  • Pre-launch audit of an existing store before a big campaign

When NOT to use

  • A single product page tweak → use frontend-dev directly
  • A pricing change on an existing product → use product-pricing-engine directly
  • A new SKU added to existing catalog → use product-catalog-builder directly
  • A bug fix → use frontend-dev / backend-dev / database-schema directly

Required inputs (one-question fallback)

If user hasn't said:

  1. What's the store? (new sub-brand / Matrix B2B portal / MuscleLock / Peptide Map / other)
  2. Existing repo or new? (if new, scaffolder runs; if existing, audit-first)
  3. Catalog size? (10 SKUs / 100 / 1K / 10K — drives schema decisions)
  4. Customer type? (B2B provider / D2C consumer / hybrid)
  5. Regulated? (controlled substances / Rx / 503-A → compliance gates needed)

If user has answered the obvious one — proceed without re-asking.

Workflow (full new build)

Phase 0 — Foundations (week 1)
  ↓ reuse-scanner (always first — what already exists for this brand?)
  ↓ ecommerce-scaffolder (bootstrap repo + base structure)
  ↓ ecommerce-schema (DB tables + RLS policies)
  ↓ ghl-eval-harness baseline (so we can detect regressions)

Phase 1 — Catalog (week 1-2)
  ↓ product-catalog-builder (taxonomy + first 10 SKUs)
  ↓ product-pricing-engine (rules + first batch)
  ↓ product-copy (titles + descriptions + SEO)
  ↓ product-imagery (hero + variants for first batch)

Phase 2 — Plumbing (week 2-3)
  ↓ ecommerce-backend (cart, checkout, order, webhook receivers)
  ↓ integration-agent → Stripe (payments + tax via Stripe Tax)
  ↓ fulfillment-shipping (Shippo connected, rate-shop wired)
  ↓ code-qa (security + correctness review)

Phase 3 — Surface (week 3-4)
  ↓ ecommerce-storefront (product, collection, cart, checkout, account UI)
  ↓ creative-director (brand + visual review)
  ↓ compliance-officer (claims + disclosure + regulated-SKU review)

Phase 4 — Ops (week 4)
  ↓ order-lifecycle-manager (refund, return, cancel flows)
  ↓ inventory-manager (stock levels + low-stock alerts)
  ↓ devops-deployment (staging + prod)

Phase 5 — Growth (week 4-5)
  ↓ ecommerce-lifecycle-marketing (abandoned cart + post-purchase + replenishment)
  ↓ analytics-architect (conversion funnel + KPI dashboard)

Phase 6 — Launch gate
  ↓ Inspection checklist (below) — every box ✅ before launch

Launch gate — inspection checklist

The store does NOT go live without every box checked:

  • Checkout smoke test passes (real card, real order, real fulfillment label, real refund)
  • Tax calculation verified in 3 states (incl. nexus state)
  • Shipping calc verified at 3 weights / 3 distances
  • Abandoned-cart workflow fires + lands in inbox
  • Order confirmation email + SMS deliver
  • Refund flow completes end-to-end (Stripe + GHL contact tagged + customer comm)
  • Inventory decrements on order; restores on refund
  • Low-stock alert fires for test SKU
  • Mobile responsive: PDP + cart + checkout pass at 375px / 768px
  • Page load < 3s LCP at p75 mobile
  • Privacy policy + terms live + linked from footer
  • CAN-SPAM footer + unsubscribe in every lifecycle email
  • Returns / refund policy live + linked
  • Regulated-SKU gates working (if applicable — provider verification, age verification, etc.)
  • Eval harness green (no regressions vs baseline)
  • Compliance-officer signoff for any health / regulated product
  • Chris approval to flip the live switch

Handoff format

When this skill orchestrates, every specialist handoff uses _Templates/handoff-template.md and includes:

  • Goal of this step
  • Inputs from previous step (with paths)
  • Acceptance criteria
  • Verification command / URL
  • Downstream consumer

Output structure

# E-commerce Build: <Store Name>

## Scope confirmed
- Brand: <sub-brand>
- Catalog size at launch: <N>
- Customer type: <B2B / D2C / hybrid>
- Regulated: <yes / no — gates needed>
- Timeline target: <date>

## Phase plan
<Markdown table — phase × specialist × ETA × output>

## Active step
**Now running**: `<specialist>` — <one-line goal>

## ✅ Completed
- <step> → <output path> → <verification result>
