Inputs from: offer-positioning, voice-call-reviewer, account-manager, sales-strategy (lost-deal reasons), analytics-architect Outputs to: engineering-lead (PRD → architecture), pmo-operator (tracking), analytics-architect (success metrics) Chains with: reuse-scanner (always before new PRDs), software-build.workflow.md

One-liner reminder

Problem → user → solution → metrics → out-of-scope. Tradeoffs surfaced. PM Skills repo conventions. Customer feedback themed (frequency × severity). Roadmap is Now/Next/Later, not dates.

skills.name: Product Naming Gate
skills.description: Mandatory gate — every product / SKU / sub-brand / label / packaging text passes through this skill before public use. Combines trademark clearance + compliance review (no implied medical claims in name) + brand voice fit + good-name filter. Returns approved / pivot / decline.
skills.intent: Catch every naming risk in one place. A product going live with a name that conflicts with trademarks OR makes an implied medical claim OR clashes with brand voice OR is undistinctive is fixable BEFORE launch and expensive AFTER. This gate is mandatory for any name reaching customers.
best_for: Pre-launch naming gate for any product / SKU / sub-brand
skills.estimated_time: Single name: 30-60 min. Batch (5-10): 2-3 hours.
skills.body_html:
Product Naming Gate

The single gate every name passes through. Trademark + compliance + brand + distinctiveness — all four must pass.

When to use

  • Pre-launch of any product / SKU / sub-brand
  • Pre-print of any label / package text
  • Marketing campaign tagline before public use
  • Sub-brand or new business unit naming
  • Renaming an existing product (rare; treat with extra care due to brand equity)

When NOT to use

  • Internal-only code names / project names
  • Already-launched name (different process — ip-counsel-coordination for any retroactive concerns)
  • Pure descriptive text that's not name-like ("5mg vial" — not a name)

The 4 gates (all must pass)

Gate 1: Trademark clearance

Via trademark-clearance skill — all 5 search layers.

Result Action
🟢 Clear Pass to next gate
🟡 Risky Counsel opinion before pass
🔴 Blocked Decline; suggest alternates
⚫ Cancelled-risk Counsel decides

Gate 2: Compliance review

Per formulation-compliance + compliance-officer + class-specific labeling rules.

Hard fails (decline):

  • Name implies medical claim ("CureFlex" / "HealAll" / "InstantRelief")
  • Name suggests FDA approval when product is compounded ("FDA-Form Plus")
  • Name appropriates regulated terminology in misleading way ("Surgical-Grade ___")
  • Name uses controlled-substance terminology for non-scheduled product ("Schedule-2 Strength")
  • Cosmetic / supplement name making drug claim ("Diabetes Defender")
  • Trademark uses claim with no substantiation ("Patented™" without patent)
  • Name includes restricted geographic indicator wrongly ("Champagne" / "Bordeaux" non-source products)

Soft flags (proceed with caution / counsel):

  • Name evokes a specific condition ("HormoneHealer" — borderline)
  • Name uses a competitor's mark suggestively
  • Name uses superlatives ("Ultimate" / "Maximum") in regulated class

Gate 3: Brand voice fit

Per brand-guide.md + sub-brands.md.

  • Sub-brand alignment: does name fit MuscleLock / Peptide Map / ClinIQ / Matrix Advanced Solutions voice?
  • Voice attributes (per sub-brands.md): does the name sound like the brand says it would sound?
  • Mark distinctiveness ladder (fanciful > arbitrary > suggestive > descriptive > generic) — Matrix sub-brand naming preference: suggestive-or-stronger

Soft-fail OK: borderline brand fit can pass if other gates strong + Chris approves.

Gate 4: Good-name filter (operational)

  • Pronounceable — voice assistant + word-of-mouth + international markets
  • Spellable — customers Googling find us
  • Domain available (.com primary; alternates acceptable but flag)
  • Social handles available on relevant platforms
  • No negative connotations in English + key foreign languages
  • No accidental phonetic collisions with banned words
  • Reasonable length — labels are tight on space
  • Memorability — would you remember it tomorrow?

Workflow

Step 1 — Intake candidate(s)

  • 1 candidate: risky (single point of failure)
  • 3-5 candidates: ideal
  • 10+ candidates: dilution; pre-filter to your top 5 first

Capture:

  • Candidate names
  • Product / use class (drug / supplement / cosmetic / SaaS / etc.)
  • Sub-brand if applicable
  • Geographic scope of use
  • Channel of use (D2C / B2B / both)
  • Launch timeline

Step 2 — Run Gate 1 (trademark clearance)

Invoke trademark-clearance skill. Output: 🟢 / 🟡 / 🔴 / ⚫ per candidate.

Step 3 — Run Gate 2 (compliance review)

Per candidate that survived Gate 1:

  • Run banned-phrase check
  • Run implied-claim check
  • Run class-specific labeling rule check
  • If 🟡 risky: compliance-officer formal review

Step 4 — Run Gate 3 (brand voice fit)

Per candidate that survived Gates 1-2:

  • Check brand-guide.md + sub-brands.md
  • Brand voice scoring (1-5)
  • Below 3: discuss with brand owner (Chris / sub-brand lead)

Step 5 — Run Gate 4 (good-name filter)

Per candidate that survived Gates 1-3:

  • Pronounceable
  • Spellable
  • Domain available
  • Social handles
  • No negative connotations
  • Memorability subjective; ideally test with 5 unbiased people

Step 6 — Score + rank

# Naming Gate — <product / sub-brand>

## Candidates assessed
| Name | Gate 1 (TM) | Gate 2 (Compliance) | Gate 3 (Brand) | Gate 4 (Good name) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|

## Recommended
- Top: <name>
- Backup: <name>

## Reasoning
<2-3 paragraphs>

## Required follow-up before public use
- File trademark application via `ip-counsel-coordination`
- Register domain
- Reserve social handles
- Label artwork incorporates ™ (if pending) or ® (if registered)

Step 7 — Approval decision

  • If all 4 gates 🟢 + Chris approves direction → proceed to commit
  • If any 🔴 → decline this candidate; surface alternates
  • If any 🟡 → escalate to counsel (Gate 1/2) or Chris (Gate 3/4) for judgment call

Step 8 — Commit

  • Hand off to ip-counsel-coordination for trademark filing
  • Hand off to product-catalog-builder to set name on SKU
  • Domain + social handle registrations (defensive)
  • Notify creative-director for branding assets

Step 9 — Post-launch monitoring

  • Quarterly: defensive trademark search (anyone else using too-similar?)
  • Annual: name still active + used (trademark use requirements)
  • If retired: document; consider abandonment vs hold-for-future

Banned phrase list (compliance Gate 2 reference)

For Matrix's regulated products, names CANNOT include or strongly imply:

  • "Cure" / "Cures" / "Curing"
  • "Heal" / "Healing" (drug context)
  • "Treat" / "Treats" / "Treatment" (for specific conditions)
  • "Prevent" / "Prevents" / "Prevention" (of disease)
  • "FDA-approved" (if not literally true)
  • "Clinically proven" (without supporting clinical trial)
  • "Medical-grade" (loose term that implies FDA)
  • "Prescription-strength" (for non-Rx)
  • "Pharmaceutical-grade" (only if literally true per USP)
  • Specific disease names (Diabetes, Cancer, Alzheimer's, etc.)
  • Hormone names paired with claim ("TestoBooster3000" — implied effect)
  • Substance + dosing ("5mg-Peak-Therapy" implies clinical use)
  • "Safe" / "Risk-free" / "No-side-effects"
  • "Guaranteed"
  • "Miracle" / "Breakthrough"
  • "Anti-aging" (drug claim in many contexts)

For supplements specifically (DSHEA):

  • May make structure/function claims with disclaimer ("supports immune function")
  • May NOT diagnose / treat / cure / prevent disease

For cosmetics:

  • Cosmetic claims OK ("moisturizes" / "softens")
  • Drug claims forbidden ("removes wrinkles permanently" = drug)

Common pitfalls

  • Name evokes a clinical condition → reads as targeting that condition → drug claim implication
  • Name uses Rx-implication wording → consumer + regulator confusion
  • Name copies competitor pattern → trademark conflict + market confusion
  • Name is too descriptive → weak trademark + customer confusion with competitors
  • Name is unpronounceable → kills word-of-mouth + voice search
  • Domain available but social handles taken → fragmented brand
  • Pre-launch use without ™ marker → weakens later trademark claim

Anti-patterns

  • Single-candidate naming — failure mode is restart from zero
  • Skipping Gate 2 (compliance) — names with implied claims often clear trademark but fail FDA / FTC
  • Marketing names approved by marketing only — needs IP + compliance + brand triad
  • Public commitment before gate — domain announcements / press leaks before clearance
  • Reusing retired product names — confusion + ghosts of past complaints
  • Generic / descriptive names — weak protection; copycats free to operate
  • Skipping defensive domain / handle registration — squatters compound after announcement

Pre-authorizations

  • ✅ Run all 4 gates
  • ✅ Rank + recommend
  • ✅ Coordinate defensive domain + handle registrations
  • ⚠️ Approve final name (Chris + sub-brand lead if applicable)
  • ⚠️ Override a soft-flag (Chris + reasoning logged)
  • 🚫 Override a hard fail in Gate 1 or Gate 2 (counsel required for Gate 1; compliance-officer required for Gate 2)
  • 🚫 Approve public use before trademark filing decision

Pairing

Inputs from: trademark-clearance (Gate 1), compliance-officer + formulation-compliance (Gate 2), brand-guide.md + sub-brands.md (Gate 3), good-name filter (Gate 4) Outputs to: ip-counsel-coordination (filing), product-catalog-builder (SKU naming), creative-director (brand assets), formulation-lead (launch gate clearance) Chains with: ip-lead (orchestrator parent), creative-director, every product launch flow

One-line reminder

4 gates: trademark + compliance + brand + good-name. All must pass. Hard fails are final. Single-candidate naming is a trap. ™ on every label until ®.

skills.name: Product Pricing Engine
skills.description: Set and manage e-commerce pricing — list price, sale price, tiered B2B pricing, promo codes, free-shipping thresholds, time-bound sales. Generates SQL to apply pricing changes and updates Stripe products if synced. Clone of `pricing-margin` adapted for catalog operations.
skills.intent: Be the only path that touches prices. Direct DB edits = audit nightmares. This skill enforces price-history, effective-dating, margin validation, and Stripe sync. Pairs with `pricing-margin` (which sets the strategy) — this skill executes it.
skills.estimated_time: Single SKU update: 5 min. Site-wide sale: 30-60 min. New B2B tier: 1-2 hours.
skills.body_html:
Product Pricing Engine

The execution layer for pricing. All price changes go through this — none directly.

Lineage

Clones: pricing-margin (strategy) Differs: this is the execution + catalog-shape skill; pricing-margin defines strategy / target margins / competitive analysis

When to use

  • New catalog needs prices
  • Sale / promo campaign
  • B2B tier launch or adjustment
  • Cost-of-goods change requires repricing
  • Stripe sync drift detected

When NOT to use

  • Pricing strategy / margin targets → use pricing-margin
  • Affiliate commission rates → use ghl-affiliate-manager
  • Per-customer custom pricing (rare, B2B only) → escalate to Chris

Required inputs

  1. Scope — single SKU / category / brand / site-wide
  2. Change kind — new price / sale / promo / tier
  3. Effective dating — when does it start? when does it end?
  4. Margin floor — what's the minimum margin we'll accept (from pricing-margin)
  5. B2B tiers? — provider tier 1/2/3 pricing differences

Pricing model

Prices table (append-only, effective-dated)

prices(id, variant_id, currency, list_cents, sale_cents?, starts_at, ends_at)

Rules:

  • Never mutate existing price rows; append new ones
  • Active price = row where starts_at <= now < ends_at (or ends_at IS NULL)
  • Old prices retained for historical reporting (refunds, disputes, tax)

Tier prices

price_tiers(id, variant_id, tier_name, min_qty, price_cents)

Tier examples (Matrix B2B):

  • tier_standard — list price (default)
  • tier_silver — 5% off list for verified providers
  • tier_gold — 10% off list for high-volume providers
  • tier_platinum — 15% off list for whale accounts

Customer's tier set via customers.tier column → applied at checkout.

Promo codes

promo_codes(id, code, kind, value_cents_or_pct, starts_at, ends_at, max_uses, used,
            min_subtotal_cents?, scoped_to_brand?, scoped_to_category?, scoped_to_variant?,
            one_per_customer)

kind: flat_off | pct_off | free_shipping | bxgy

Rules:

  • Cannot stack unless explicitly flagged
  • Validate at checkout server-side (never client-side)
  • Use exhaustion is atomic (UPDATE...WHERE used < max_uses RETURNING)

Workflow

Step 1 — Confirm scope + change

  • One-question fallback if unclear
  • Load current prices for affected variants
  • Compute proposed margin per variant
  • Flag any variant going below margin floor

Step 2 — Apply margin check

  • For every proposed price: compute (price - cogs) / price
  • If below pricing-margin floor → escalate to Chris before proceeding
  • If above floor → continue

Step 3 — Generate price changes

  • Build SQL with new price rows + effective dating
  • For sales: set sale_cents on existing or new row; set ends_at
  • For tiered: build per-tier rows
  • For promo: build promo_codes row

Step 4 — Dry run + preview

  • Output: variant × old price × new price × effective period × margin
  • Customer-impact preview: how does the home page / category page look with new prices
  • Stripe sync preview: which Stripe Products / Prices will be created/updated

Step 5 — Chris approval (always for prod)

  • Show preview
  • Get explicit approval
  • Capture in _Logs/pricing/<date>-<scope>-approval.md

Step 6 — Apply

  • Run SQL in transaction
  • Sync to Stripe (create/update Products + Prices via Stripe API)
  • Invalidate cache (CDN + storefront ISR)
  • Verify storefront reflects change in < 60s

Step 7 — Notify

  • If sale → trigger marketing comms via ecommerce-lifecycle-marketing (if planned)
  • If price increase → notify long-time customers per account-manager policy

Step 8 — Monitor

  • For first 24h: track conversion + AOV delta
  • Alert if conversion drops > 20% (indicates pricing too aggressive)
  • Roll back if confirmed regression

Step 9 — Log

  • Append entry to _Logs/pricing/<YYYY-MM>/changes.csv:
    • variant_id, old, new, margin, effective period, approver, reason

Margin guardrails

Channel Min margin
D2C list price 60%
D2C sale price 50% (rare drop allowed by Chris)
B2B tier_standard 45%
B2B tier_gold 40%
B2B tier_platinum 35%
Promo (any) 30% absolute floor — never less

Override path: Chris approval + reason logged. No exceptions.

Stripe sync

If catalog syncs to Stripe (Products + Prices for Stripe-hosted checkout):

  • One Stripe Product per variant (NOT per product — Stripe's "Product" maps to our "Variant")
  • One Stripe Price per (variant, currency, kind) — never reprice; deactivate + create new
  • Sale price = separate active Stripe Price; revert by deactivating sale Price
  • Promo codes = Stripe Promotion Codes if checking out via Stripe Checkout; else applied server-side before Stripe call

Anti-patterns

  • Editing prices directly in admin / DB — no audit, no history, no Stripe sync
  • Mutating existing price rows — append, never mutate
  • Sale with no ends_at — sale that never ends is just the new price (and you'll forget)
  • Margin override with no approver logged — accountability gap
  • Client-side promo validation — guaranteed abuse
  • Stripe sync skipped — checkout will use stale prices
  • Promo stacking enabled by default — too easy to give the store away
  • Cents stored as float — always integer

Pre-authorizations

  • ✅ Compute margins + propose price changes
  • ✅ Apply price changes to staging
  • ⚠️ Apply to prod (Chris approval per change)
  • ⚠️ Margin override below floor (Chris + reason)
  • ⚠️ Site-wide sale > 20% off (Chris + marketing-aware)
  • 🚫 Custom per-customer pricing (Chris only)

Pairing

Inputs from: pricing-margin (strategy + margin floors), product-catalog-builder (variants), ecommerce-schema (tables), bookkeeping (cogs reference) Outputs to: ecommerce-storefront (display), ecommerce-backend (cart calc), ecommerce-lifecycle-marketing (sale campaigns), analytics-architect (price-elasticity reports) Chains with: ghl-affiliate-manager (commission base = pre-discount or post-discount per terms)

One-line reminder

Append, never mutate. Margin floor enforced. Effective dating mandatory. Stripe in sync. Every change has an approver and a reason.

skills.name: Project Overseer
skills.description: Scan all Claude projects under ~/Projects/, report status across them, surface stale ones, identify cross-project patterns. Use when the user wants oversight across multiple projects, not just AI Company. Runs in weekly-review by default.
skills.body_html:
Project Overseer

Cross-project visibility. Scan all projects with a CLAUDE.md, surface status, find stale work.

Purpose

The user has many Claude projects. Each has its own context. Without oversight, projects drift — work stalls, focus shifts, nothing wraps. This skill provides the bird's-eye view.

When to use

  • Weekly review (auto-included)
  • Before starting a new project (check what's already in flight)
  • When the user feels "what am I even working on right now"
  • Quarterly portfolio review

When NOT to use

  • Deep dive on one project (cd into it and use that project's skills)
  • Project setup (use claude-md-builder or init skill)

Required setup

  • Filesystem read access to ~/Projects/ (or specified parent dir)
  • Git (for last-activity detection)

Workflow

Step 1 — Discover projects

find ~/Projects -maxdepth 3 -name "CLAUDE.md" -not -path "*/node_modules/*" -not -path "*/.git/*"

Each match is a "project under your watch."

Step 2 — For each project, capture status

cd <project>

# Last activity
git log -1 --format="%ai %s" 2>/dev/null

# Last file modification (non-git)
find . -type f -not -path "*/.git/*" -not -path "*/node_modules/*" -printf '%TY-%Tm-%Td %p\n' | sort -r | head -1

# Open tasks
[ -f TASKS.md ] && grep -E "^- \[ \]" TASKS.md | wc -l

# Stale waiting items
[ -f TASKS.md ] && grep -A 100 "## Waiting on" TASKS.md | grep -E "^- \[ \]" | wc -l

# Recent commits this week
git log --since="7 days ago" --oneline 2>/dev/null | wc -l

# Project focus (first paragraph of CLAUDE.md if present)
head -10 CLAUDE.md

Step 3 — Classify each project

Status Criteria
🟢 Active Activity in last 7 days, low waiting count
🟡 Slowing Activity in last 14 days but trending down
🔴 Stale No activity in 14+ days
⚫ Dormant No activity in 60+ days — propose archive or revival
⚠️ Blocked "Waiting on" items > 3, or all open tasks waiting

Step 4 — Cross-project patterns

Look for:

  • Same person mentioned across projects (recurring dependency / champion)
  • Same blocker across projects (e.g., "waiting on legal" everywhere)
  • Same tech stack issue (build error, dep upgrade) — could be fixed once across all
  • Drift — project has a focus statement but recent work doesn't match it
  • Skill reuse opportunity — same workflow built in 2 projects, should be a shared skill

Step 5 — Output portfolio report

Output structure

# Project Oversight — <Date>

**Total projects**: <N>
**Active**: 🟢 <N>  |  Slowing: 🟡 <N>  |  Stale: 🔴 <N>  |  Dormant: ⚫ <N>  |  Blocked: ⚠️ <N>

---

## 🟢 Active

### <Project Name> (`~/Projects/<name>/`)
- **Focus** (from CLAUDE.md): <one-liner>
- **Last activity**: <date> — <last commit msg>
- **This week**: <N commits / file changes>
- **Open tasks**: <N> (P0: <X>, P1: <Y>)
- **Waiting on**: <N items>
- **Health**: ✅ on track / ⚠️ minor drift
- **Next milestone**: <if stated in PLANS.md / TASKS.md>

[Repeat per Active project]

---

## 🟡 Slowing (needs nudge)

### <Project Name>
- **Last activity**: <date — N days ago>
- **Why slowing**: <hypothesis: blocked / lost interest / pivoted>
- **Suggested action**: <commit / kill / re-scope>

---

## 🔴 Stale (14+ days no activity)

### <Project Name>
- **Last activity**: <date>
- **What was the last thing done**: <commit msg or note>
- **Suggested action**:
  - [ ] Resume + commit a next step
  - [ ] Pause formally (move to "paused" status in CLAUDE.md)
  - [ ] Kill (archive folder)

---

## ⚫ Dormant (60+ days)

| Project | Last activity | Suggested action |
|---|---|---|
| <name> | <date> | Archive |

---

## ⚠️ Blocked

### <Project Name>
- **Blocker**: <waiting on what or whom>
- **Waiting since**: <date>
- **Suggested unblock**: <ping the person / escalate / drop>

---

## 🔁 Cross-project patterns

### Recurring people
- <Person>: mentioned in <N projects> — common dependency
- <Person>: appearing across <projects> — possible team consolidation

### Recurring blockers
- "Waiting on legal" in <N projects> — bulk follow-up?
- "Awaiting API key" in <N projects> — provision once?

### Tech drift
- <Project A> and <Project B> both on outdated <library X> — bulk upgrade window?

### Skill reuse opportunity
- <Project A> built a custom <thing>; <Project B> needs same — extract into shared skill?

---

## Delegation

### ✅ I'll handle
- Update project status flags in each CLAUDE.md
- Move dormant projects' folders to `~/Projects/_Archive/`  (after your approval)
- Cross-link related projects in CLAUDE.md "see also" sections
- Update this AI Company project's TASKS.md with cross-project follow-ups

### ⚠️ Need from you
- Decide kill vs revive on <N> stale/dormant projects
- Decide if <person/blocker pattern> warrants a consolidated action
- Approve archive moves

### 🚫 You only
- Personal calls / pings to unblock waiting items
- Any kill decisions on client / paying work

Anti-patterns

  • Counting everything as "active" — be honest. Stale is stale.
  • Auto-archiving dormant — always ⚠️ approve first
  • Reporting on too many projects — > 20 projects = skip dormant in default output
  • Surfacing patterns that aren't real patterns — 2 instances ≠ pattern, need 3+
  • No "suggested action" per stale project — that's the whole value

Pre-authorizations

  • Read access to all ~/Projects/*/ folders
  • Read access to git history in each
  • Update last-checked timestamp in each project's CLAUDE.md
  • NEVER auto-archive or delete project folders — always ⚠️

Pairing

  • Auto-runs as part of weekly-review
  • Pairs with task-list-manager (cross-project task aggregation)
  • Pairs with automation-scheduler (auto-run weekly)

What "project" means

A folder under ~/Projects/ (or wherever Chris's projects live) that:

  • Has a CLAUDE.md at root, OR
  • Has a README.md declaring it as an active project, OR
  • Has been opted in via ~/Projects/.claude-projects.list (user-maintained whitelist)

Otherwise, skip the folder.

One-liner reminder

Scan all projects. Classify. Surface stale ones. Find cross-project patterns. Approve before any destructive action.

skills.name: Project Scaffolder
skills.description: Bootstrap a new project — folder structure, CLAUDE.md, TASKS.md, README.md, project-brief.md, .gitignore, env stub. Picks the right scaffold per project type (Next.js web, Python service, CLI tool, content site, GHL automation pack, docs). For Matrix: clones from the matrix-store / matrix-advanced-solutions patterns.
best_for: Starting any new project longer than a day
Cloning the Matrix project pattern for a new vertical / sub-brand
Standardizing onboarding for new client projects
skills.estimated_time: 5-10 min
skills.body_html:
Project Scaffolder

Cut the project-setup tax. Every new project starts with: scaffold → commit → start building.

When to use

  • Starting any new project that will live for more than a day
  • Cloning a successful project pattern for a new use case (new vertical / sub-brand)
  • Setting up a folder to experiment in a structured way

When NOT to use

  • Personal scratch notes (just write a markdown file)
  • Existing project you're contributing to (don't re-scaffold)
  • One-off script (a single .py or .sh file is enough)

Required inputs

  1. Project name — kebab-case, descriptive
  2. Project type — pick from the type matrix below
  3. Parent folder — typically ~/Projects/
  4. Owner — who's accountable (default: Chris)
  5. Brand-bound? — yes (uses Matrix brand-identity) / no (neutral)

If 2 is unclear, ask: "What type of project — Next.js web, Python service, CLI tool, content site, GHL automation pack, docs, or other?"

Project type matrix

Type Scaffold Common files
next-web Next.js 16 + Tailwind v4 + TypeScript (Matrix default stack) app/, components/, lib/, data/, public/, package.json, tailwind.config.ts, tsconfig.json
next-app-router Same as above but emphasizes Server Components by default Per Matrix CLAUDE.md architecture rules
python-service FastAPI + Pydantic + pytest app/, tests/, requirements.txt, Dockerfile
cli-tool Node CLI or Python CLI src/, bin/, package.json or pyproject.toml
content-site Markdown content + Astro / 11ty content/, src/, public/
ghl-automation GHL workflow exports + docs workflows/, templates/, docs/, automations.md
docs-site Mintlify / Docusaurus docs/, mint.json or docusaurus.config.js
claude-skill-pack Skills folder matching the company-OS pattern skills/, README.md, CLAUDE.md
research-folder Markdown notes + asset folders notes/, assets/, references/
fresh Bare minimum (CLAUDE.md, TASKS.md, README.md, .gitignore) Catch-all

For each type, the scaffolder fills in template files appropriate to that stack.

Workflow

Step 1 — Verify parent + name doesn't conflict

ls ~/Projects/<name> 2>/dev/null && echo "EXISTS — pick different name or use a different parent"

Stop if exists.

Step 2 — Pick the scaffold

From the type matrix. For Matrix-bound projects, default to next-web (matches matrix-store / matrix-advanced-solutions).

Step 3 — Generate the folder structure

Create the folder tree per the type. For next-web (most common for Matrix):

mkdir -p <name>/{app,components,lib,data,public}
mkdir -p <name>/components/{ui,sections}
mkdir -p <name>/lib/{forms,analytics,automation}
mkdir -p <name>/.claude

Step 4 — Generate the standard files

Every project (regardless of type) gets:

CLAUDE.md — auto-generated per project context:

# <Project Name>

## Project
<one-line: what this project is>

## Owner
<owner>

## Brand
<brand-bound: link to Matrix brand-identity OR neutral>

## Tech stack
<per type>

## Commands
<scaffold-specific run / build / test commands>

## Architecture rules
<per type>

## Compliance / safety
<per project: HIPAA flag, PHI handling, A2P, etc.>

## Status
<active / paused / dormant>
**Last updated**: <date>

TASKS.md — empty 5-section structure (Today / This week / Backlog / Waiting on / Done): Per _Skills Library/KB-Skills/task-list-manager/SKILL.md format.

README.md — concise:

# <Project Name>

<one-line description>

## What's shipped
- (empty)

## Run locally
<commands per type>

## Deploy
<per type>

## Status
<status>

project-brief.md — uses _Templates/project-brief.md: Pre-fill: owner, start date, project name. Leave problem-statement + KPIs as placeholders for user.

.gitignore — per type:

  • Node: standard Node + Next.js
  • Python: standard Python
  • All: .env*, .DS_Store, IDE files, logs

.env.example — placeholder env vars per stack.

.claude/settings.local.json — empty allowlist (user adds permissions as needed).

Step 5 — Stack-specific files

For next-web:

  • package.json (Next.js 16, React 19, Tailwind v4, Framer Motion, RHF, Zod, Lucide, clsx, tailwind-merge)
  • tsconfig.json (strict mode, paths to @/*)
  • tailwind.config.ts (Matrix dark-first palette if brand-bound)
  • next.config.ts (Server Components default, image optimization)
  • app/layout.tsx (Inter font, base layout)
  • app/page.tsx (hello-world)
  • app/globals.css (Tailwind base + CSS variables for brand colors if brand-bound)

For python-service:

  • pyproject.toml (or requirements.txt)
  • app/__init__.py, app/main.py (FastAPI hello-world)
  • tests/__init__.py, tests/test_main.py
  • Dockerfile (if production-bound)

For ghl-automation:

  • automations.md (registry of all automations)
  • workflows/ (one .md per workflow design)
  • templates/email/, templates/sms/
  • docs/setup.md
  • data/contacts.csv (sample structure)

Step 6 — git init + initial commit

cd <name>
git init
git add .
git commit -m "scaffold: initial project structure"

Step 7 — Register in PMO

Update _Shared Context/agent-registry.md "Related projects" section (or wherever projects are listed).

Add to pmo-operator master board on next invocation.

Step 8 — Output the scaffold report

Output structure

# Project Scaffolded: <name>

## Location
`~/Projects/<name>/`

## Type
<type>

## Files created
- `<name>/CLAUDE.md`
- `<name>/TASKS.md`
- `<name>/README.md`
- `<name>/project-brief.md`
- `<name>/.gitignore`
- `<name>/.env.example`
- (per-type files listed)

## Git status
- Initialized
- Initial commit made

## Next steps
1. `cd ~/Projects/<name>`
2. `<install command per stack — npm install / pip install / etc.>`
3. Fill `project-brief.md` problem statement + KPIs
4. Start building

## Registered
- ✅ Added to projects list in `agent-registry.md`
- ✅ Will appear in next `project-overseer` run

## Logged to
`_Logs/agent-decisions/<date>-scaffold-<name>.md`

Anti-patterns

  • Generic scaffolds when a Matrix project pattern exists — clone the pattern instead
  • No CLAUDE.md — future agents won't know what the project is
  • No TASKS.md — task-list-manager + pmo-operator can't track
  • No .gitignore for the stack — secrets leak risk
  • Skipping project-brief.md for projects > 1 week — drift starts immediately
  • Pre-installing every possible dependency — use the minimum for the stack

Pre-authorizations

  • ✅ Create folder + standard files
  • ✅ git init + initial commit
  • ✅ Register in projects list
  • ⚠️ Install dependencies (npm install / pip install) — Chris approves first run
  • 🚫 Push to remote (manual on first push)

Pairing

Inputs from:

  • User project description
  • _Shared Context/company-profile.md (for Matrix-bound projects)
  • _Shared Context/brand-guide.md (for brand-bound styling)
  • Existing Matrix projects for cloning pattern (matrix-store, matrix-advanced-solutions)

Outputs to:

  • New project folder
  • agent-registry.md projects list
  • pmo-operator (for tracking)
  • claude-md-builder if more elaborate CLAUDE.md needed (overlap — this skill handles basic)

One-liner reminder

Type → scaffold → standard files → git init → register. Don't reinvent; clone Matrix's stack defaults for brand-bound work.

skills.name: Prompt Engineer
skills.description: Author or refine a system prompt for an agent. Applies the frameworks from _Knowledge Base/03 (C.R.E.A.T.E., S.P.E.C.IFY, anatomy of a strong system prompt). Outputs a ready-to-paste prompt + a 3-5-round refinement plan.
skills.body_html:
Prompt Engineer

Author or refine a system prompt. Apply the C.R.E.A.T.E. / S.P.E.C.IFY frameworks from _Knowledge Base/03 - Prompt Engineering Playbook.md.

Purpose

Stop improvising system prompts. Use the canonical anatomy + iterate over 3-5 rounds.

When to use

  • After agent-build-plan returns and you need the prompt
  • Rewriting an existing prompt that's under-performing
  • Authoring tool descriptions for an agent

When NOT to use

  • One-shot user prompts (just write them)
  • Documentation / general writing (different skill)

Required inputs

  1. Agent purpose — one sentence
  2. Target audience — who will the agent talk to / serve
  3. Tools (if tool-using) — list with read/write
  4. Constraints — what the agent must NOT do
  5. Output format expected — markdown / JSON / structured / freeform

If 1 or 2 is missing, ask ONE question: "Who is this agent for, and what's the one job it has to do?"

Workflow

Step 1 — Apply the canonical anatomy

Every strong system prompt has these sections (in order):

  1. Role — who/what the agent is
  2. Purpose — single-sentence job
  3. Context — what they need to know about the user/system
  4. Instructions — what to DO (numbered steps)
  5. Constraints — what NOT to do (banned words, refusal triggers, edge cases)
  6. Examples — 1-3 input → output samples
  7. Output format — schema or template

Step 2 — Apply C.R.E.A.T.E. (Mark — Prompt God)

For each section, check:

  • Character (persona consistent?)
  • Request (specific ask, not vague?)
  • Examples (1-3 worked, not 0 or 10?)
  • Adjustments (refinement hooks for iteration?)
  • Type (output type explicit?)
  • Extras (chain-of-thought, JSON schema, tool list, etc.)

Step 3 — Plan 3-5 refinement rounds (Innanen)

The first draft is never the final. Plan the rounds:

  • Round 1: First draft. Test on 3 representative inputs.
  • Round 2: Patch failures. Add few-shots for the failed patterns.
  • Round 3: Tighten constraints. Add refusal/escalation triggers.
  • Round 4: Compress. Cut anything not load-bearing.
  • Round 5: Lock. Version it. Don't change without an eval.

Step 4 — Output

Two artifacts:

  1. The system prompt (ready to paste)
  2. The 3-5-round refinement plan with test inputs

Output structure

# System Prompt: <agent name>

## Version 1 (Draft)

You are .

Purpose

Context

Instructions

...

Constraints

Examples

Input

Input

Output format

<schema / template>


## Refinement plan

| Round | Goal | Test inputs | Pass criteria |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | First draft works | <3 inputs> | <pass criteria> |
| 2 | Patch failures | <new inputs> | <pass criteria> |
| 3 | Add guardrails | <edge cases> | <pass criteria> |
| 4 | Compress | <same inputs> | <output stays correct, prompt shorter> |
| 5 | Lock | <full eval set> | <eval pass> |

## Anti-patterns to avoid

- **"You are a helpful assistant."** — too generic. Be specific.
- **Listing 30 instructions** — agents lose track past ~10. Group or compress.
- **Vague constraints** — "be safe" is not a constraint. "Never claim X" is.
- **No examples** — agents extrapolate poorly without 1-3 few-shots.
- **No output format** — agents invent structure inconsistently.

## Open questions
- <if any>

Anti-patterns (in the skill itself)

  • Skipping the refinement plan — first draft is never final
  • Adding constraints without examples — agents follow examples more reliably than rules
  • Too many examples — 1-3 is the sweet spot
  • Mixing instructions and constraints — keep separate (DO vs DON'T)
  • No version number — every locked prompt needs a version

Chain

→ Feeds into agent-build-plan Phase 2 output. → Pair with eval-harness-builder (future skill) for the refinement loop.

One-liner reminder

Anatomy + C.R.E.A.T.E. + 3-5 rounds. Never lock a prompt without an eval pass.

skills.name: Proposal Builder
skills.description: Generate customer-specific proposals, quotes, and packages. Sales-stage gap between discovery and close. Inputs the customer's discovery notes + offer + pricing; outputs a tailored proposal doc (PDF or markdown) with scope, pricing, terms, next steps. For Matrix: clinic-specific scope + offer mix + payment terms.
best_for: Quote for specific product / bundle / volume
Renewal proposals with expansion line items
skills.estimated_time: 15-30 min per proposal
skills.body_html:
Proposal Builder

The sales-stage gap-filler. Discovery → proposal → close. Tailored, on-brand, never auto-sent.

When to use

  • After discovery-call-prep informed a discovery call
  • Quote for a specific product / volume request
  • Renewal with expansion line items
  • Multi-stakeholder proposals (different sections per buyer)

When NOT to use

  • Catalog request only (use cold-email-sequence catalog template)
  • Generic info (no customer-specific tailoring needed)
  • Verbal close (no formal proposal needed)

Required inputs

  1. Customer / contact — GHL contact ID
  2. Discovery notes — captured during the call (pain, scope, pricing band, decision criteria)
  3. Offer(s) being proposed — specific products / bundles / tiers (from _Shared Context/offers.md)
  4. Pricing — from pricing-margin if non-standard, else from offers.md
  5. Stakeholders — who's on the other side (decision-maker, user, blocker, champion)
  6. Timeline — when they want to start, decision date

If 2 missing, ask: "Paste the discovery notes (pain, scope, pricing band, criteria) or link to the GHL contact note."

Workflow

Step 1 — Load context

  • _Shared Context/company-profile.md (positioning)
  • _Shared Context/offers.md (the offer)
  • _Shared Context/brand-guide.md (voice)
  • _Shared Context/compliance-rules.md (claims discipline)
  • Individual Skills/brand-identity.md (visual)
  • GHL contact + discovery notes

Step 2 — Tailor the scope to customer pain

Map discovery findings → offer specifics:

  • Pain mentioned → product / bundle that solves it
  • Volume mentioned → tier / pricing
  • Timing mentioned → start date in proposal
  • Objection raised → addressed in proposal section
  • Wedge / FOMU → highlighted

Step 3 — Pick the proposal structure

For Matrix B2B clinic proposals:

1. Cover (1 page)
   - Customer name + clinic
   - Proposal date + valid-through date
   - Matrix logo + tagline

2. Executive summary (1 paragraph)
   - The specific outcome this proposal delivers
   - The customer's pain it addresses (mirror their language)
   - The recommended path

3. The scope (1-2 pages)
   - What's included (specific products / bundles / services)
   - What's NOT included (intentional exclusions — sets expectations)
   - Quantities / volumes
   - Service-layer (account manager, onboarding, education access)

4. Pricing (1 page)
   - Line items with quantities + unit price + total
   - Bundle discount if applicable
   - Payment terms (Immediate / EOD / EOW / Net 30)
   - Volume tier reached / not reached

5. Terms (1 page)
   - Provider-only access reminder
   - Credentialing prerequisite
   - Shipping + fulfillment SLAs
   - Returns / replacement policy
   - Compliance disclaimers

6. Implementation timeline (1 page)
   - Week 1: credentialing verification
   - Week 2: first order placement + onboarding kickoff
   - Week 3-4: first delivery + 14-day check-in
   - Week 4-8: ramp + 30-day milestone
   - Month 3: QBR

7. Next steps (1 paragraph)
   - "Sign + return + place first order"
   - E-sign link
   - Calendar link for any open questions
   - Account manager contact

8. About Matrix (1 page — for new buyers; skip for existing)
   - One-paragraph company overview
   - Wedge / differentiators
   - Proof / case studies (if approved for use)
   - Compliance / credentialing posture

9. Appendix (optional)
   - Detailed product specs
   - BAA template (if PHI-bearing)
   - Affiliate program info (if relevant)

For SaaS / service-only proposals, swap "Pricing" structure to per-seat / per-task / outcome-based per the offer.

Step 4 — Apply brand voice

Per brand-guide.md:

  • Authoritative, clinical, peer-level
  • No exclamation marks
  • No consumer tone
  • Compliance phrases where context demands
  • Speaks to providers as colleagues

Step 5 — Apply Hawkins discipline

  • Prescriptive narrowing: one recommended path, not a menu (+86% purchase ease)
  • FOMU framing: surface the unique-only-Matrix value, not feature parity
  • Specific next step: one clear action ("Sign + place first order by [date]"), not "let us know"

Step 6 — Generate the proposal

Output formats:

  • Markdown (.md) — default, easy to edit
  • PDF — final-form for signing
  • Branded HTML — for fancy presentation
  • Slide deck (via slide-deck-builder if customer prefers visual)

For Matrix's site/brand, use the cinematic palette (navy/steel) for PDF/HTML proposals.

Step 7 — Compliance check (via compliance-qa)

  • No medical claims beyond approved language
  • No FDA-approval claims (unless literally true)
  • No guaranteed outcomes
  • Provider-only language present
  • Testimonials (if any) have documented permission
  • Pricing matches authoritative source (no fictional numbers)

Step 8 — Output for review

Output structure

# Proposal Generated: <Customer Name>

## Spec
**Customer**: <clinic>
**Decision-maker**: <name + role>
**Discovery date**: <date>
**Proposal date**: <date>
**Valid through**: <date — typically 30 days>
**Offer(s)**: <list>
**Total proposed value**: $<X>
**Format**: <markdown / PDF / HTML / slide deck>

## Proposal file
**Path**: `<path to generated proposal>`

## Compliance check
- [x] Run through `compliance-qa`
- [x] Pricing verified against `_Shared Context/offers.md`
- [x] Brand voice match
- [x] No banned phrases / unsubstantiated claims
- [x] Provider-only language present

## Personalization audit
- [x] Customer's specific pain mirrored in exec summary
- [x] Scope aligns with discovery findings
- [x] Objections raised in discovery are addressed
- [x] Timeline matches customer's stated start date
- [x] One clear next step

## Tailoring decisions
| Discovery signal | Tailored response in proposal |
|---|---|
| <pain> | <section that addresses it> |
| <objection> | <section that addresses it> |
| <volume hint> | <pricing tier reflected> |

## ✅ I handled
- Generated the proposal draft
- Tailored to discovery findings
- Compliance pass
- Format conversion (PDF / etc.)

## ⚠️ Need from you (Chris / AE)
- Review the proposal before sending
- Approve any non-standard pricing / terms
- Decide on inclusion of specific case studies / testimonials
- Send to customer (via DocuSign / PandaDoc / email)

## 🚫 You only
- Sign final proposal (you, not me)
- Approve any contract modifications customer requests
- Negotiate price changes outside approved bands

## Handoff
**To**:
- Chris / AE (review + send)
- E-sign system (DocuSign / PandaDoc / etc.)
- `ghl-crm-architect` (opportunity moves to "Proposal Sent" stage)
- `account-manager` (prepares onboarding if closed-won)

## Logged to
`_Logs/agent-decisions/<date>-proposal-<customer>.md`

Anti-patterns

  • Generic proposals that ignore discovery findings — kills conversion
  • Multiple recommended paths — Hawkins says one, not menu
  • No expiration date — proposals without urgency stall
  • Auto-sending the proposal — never. Always Chris / AE review first.
  • Inconsistent pricing vs _Shared Context/offers.md — fix the source first
  • Promising outcomes beyond approved language
  • No "what's NOT included" — expectation drift

Pre-authorizations

  • ✅ Generate proposal draft
  • ✅ Run compliance-qa pass
  • ✅ Format conversion (markdown → PDF / HTML)
  • ✅ Update GHL opportunity to "Proposal Generated" stage
  • ⚠️ Send to customer (Chris / AE approves)
  • ⚠️ Update opportunity to "Proposal Sent"
  • 🚫 Sign / commit on behalf
  • 🚫 Negotiate / modify pricing without approval
  • 🚫 Approve refund / credit clauses

Pairing

Inputs from: discovery-call-prep (notes), offer-positioning (Offer Brief), pricing-margin (custom pricing), _Shared Context/offers.md Outputs to: Chris / AE (review), e-sign system, ghl-crm-architect (opportunity stage update), account-manager (if closed-won) Chains with: sales-strategy (proposal stage in close path), customer-onboarding (if closed-won)

One-liner reminder

Tailor to discovery. One recommended path. Specific next step. Compliance-passed. Brand-voiced. Never auto-sent. Pricing matches offers.md.

skills.name: Qbr Prep
skills.description: Quarterly Business Review prep for every white-glove + enterprise account — usage trends, outcomes delivered, roadmap preview, expansion options, open issues. AM + Chris review + present. Per `_Templates/micro-agent.template.md`.
skills.intent: QBR is the moment Matrix proves ongoing value + earns the next year. Without disciplined prep, QBRs become status meetings instead of strategic ones. This specialist produces a publishable brief T-7 days every quarter.
best_for: Quarterly QBR brief per white-glove / enterprise account
skills.estimated_time: Per brief: 60-90 min. Quarterly batch: 1-2 days for all white-glove/enterprise.
skills.body_html:
QBR Prep

Quarterly Business Review brief generator. Per _Templates/micro-agent.template.md.

ROLE

Generates the QBR brief for each white-glove + enterprise account, T-7 days before the scheduled QBR. Pulls customer-defined success (from onboarding), trailing-quarter usage + order trends, outcomes delivered (qualitative + quantitative), open issues + status, roadmap items relevant to this account, expansion options sized + qualified, NPS + CSAT trajectory, and competitive context. Outputs a 4-6 page brief + 1-slide exec summary. AM + Chris review T-3, present T-0.

Explicitly OUT of scope: Running the QBR meeting (AM + Chris), generating slides (slides skill), expansion outreach (expansion-playbook-runner), per-account financial close (bookkeeping).

INPUT

  • Trigger: quarterly-cron (28d before quarter end) / AM manual / per-account
  • account_id (single) OR all-eligible (tier ∈ {white-glove, enterprise})
  • QBR date scheduled in GHL calendar

RULES

  • Eligibility: tier white-glove OR enterprise (smaller accounts get lightweight digest, not QBR brief)
  • Delivered T-7 days minimum (gives AM + Chris time to review + edit)
  • Outcomes section MUST tie back to customer-defined success captured at kickoff (from onboarding workflow)
  • Open issues surfaced honestly (no spin)
  • All customer-facing content (exec summary, slide) passes compliance-officer
  • Trade-secret discipline (no ALM DT parameters, no proprietary formulation IP)
  • PHI/PII discipline — any aggregated patient/outcome data in customer's clinic redacted per pii-redactor
  • Expansion options sized realistically (ACV lift + effort + timing)
  • Roadmap preview: only public-OK items (no IP leaks; clear with ip-lead if uncertain)
  • Sub-brand respected — clinic-of-clinics with mixed Matrix + MuscleLock needs both voices represented

TOOLS

  • GHL MCP (read contact + opps + custom fields + conversations + calendar)
  • Supabase (order history + trends)
  • Stripe (revenue + payment health)
  • Compose: account-manager, customer-health-scoring, expansion-playbook-runner (sized options), compliance-officer, pii-redactor, analytics-architect, slides (exec summary slide)
  • Read: customer-defined success from _Logs/customer-onboarding/<account_id>/, products.md, offers.md, sub-brands.md, public roadmap (per pmo-operator)
  • Write: _Logs/cs/qbr-briefs/<account_id>/<quarter>.md + 1-slide exec summary

OUTPUT

output:
  schema:
    qbr_brief_id: string
    account_id: string
    tier: white-glove | enterprise
    quarter: string  # e.g., "2026-Q2"
    qbr_scheduled: ISO
    brief_delivered_at: ISO  # T-7 minimum
    sections:
      executive_summary:
        one_paragraph: string
        health_status: string
        key_wins: array
        key_open_issues: array
      customer_defined_success_scorecard:
        - success_criterion: string  # verbatim from kickoff
          delivered: yes | partial | not-yet
          evidence: string
      quarter_in_numbers:
        orders_placed: number
        revenue: number
        revenue_delta_qoq: number
        nps_current: number
        csat_current: number
        active_skus: number
        support_tickets: number
      outcomes_delivered:
        - outcome: string  # qualitative
          evidence: string
      open_issues:
        - issue: string
          status: string
          owner: string
          resolution_target: ISO
      roadmap_preview:
        - item: string
          relevance_to_customer: string
          public_OK: boolean
      expansion_options:
        - option: string
          rationale: string
          estimated_acv_lift: number
          effort: small | medium | large
      competitive_context:
        notes: string
        named_competitors_mentioned: array
    review_status:
      am_reviewed_at: ISO | null
      chris_reviewed_at: ISO | null
      compliance_approved: boolean
    presentation_artifacts:
      exec_summary_slide: path
      full_brief_md: path
  format: Markdown brief + 1 slide via `slides` skill

HANDOFF

  • account-manager (AM reviews T-3 days, edits, owns presentation)
  • Chris (reviews T-3 days, joins QBR T-0 for white-glove + enterprise)
  • compliance-officer (gates exec summary + slide)
  • expansion-playbook-runner (sized options feed back)
  • customer-success-lead (QBR completion rate KPI)
  • Post-QBR action items → pmo-operator ticket queue

Eval cases

  1. Enterprise QBR brief delivered T-7 days → AM + Chris review T-3 → presented T-0
  2. White-glove QBR — mixed sub-brand customer → both voices represented
  3. Open issue surfaced honestly (e.g., delayed shipment cohort) → handled in QBR
  4. Outcomes-vs-customer-defined-success: 3/5 delivered → acknowledged + plan
  5. Expansion options 3 sized → 1 accepted in QBR → expansion-playbook-runner chain
  6. Roadmap item flagged "needs ip-lead clearance" → escalated before brief publish
  7. PII redaction — patient outcome aggregates redacted to clinic-level stats
  8. QBR completed → action items routed to pmo-operator

Anti-patterns

  • Last-minute brief (delivered day-of, AM can't prep)
  • No scorecard tie-back to customer-defined success (loses thread)
  • Cherry-picked outcomes (omit open issues → trust erodes)
  • Roadmap leak (IP-sensitive items shared)
  • Same brief template for white-glove vs enterprise (enterprise needs deeper financial + strategic framing)
  • Slides without brief (no document to leave behind)

Pre-authorizations

  • ✅ Pull data + compose brief
  • ✅ Generate exec summary slide
  • ⚠️ Roadmap preview publish (ip-lead clearance for sensitive items)
  • ⚠️ Expansion options framing (AM + Chris)
  • 🚫 Make pricing commitment in brief (Chris-only)
  • 🚫 Disclose trade-secret formulation params

Pairing

Composed by: customer-success-lead, account-manager Composes: customer-health-scoring, expansion-playbook-runner, compliance-officer, pii-redactor, analytics-architect, slides Reads: GHL, Supabase, Stripe, customer-defined success from onboarding logs, products.md, offers.md Outputs to: AMs, Chris, compliance-officer, expansion-playbook-runner, pmo-operator

One-liner reminder

T-7 minimum. Customer-defined success the spine. Outcomes + open issues honest. Roadmap public-OK. Expansion sized. Compliance-gated. White-glove + enterprise only.

skills.name: Quick Research
skills.description: Research any topic from multiple sources and deliver a clean summary report
skills.body_html:
Quick Research

You are a research assistant. When the user asks you to research a topic, follow these steps exactly.

Step 1: Understand the Scope

Figure out what the user actually needs:

  • Quick answer — "What's the deal with [thing]?" = 2-3 paragraph summary
  • Comparison — "Should I use X or Y?" = side-by-side analysis
  • Deep dive — "Dig into [topic]" = full report with sources

Default to a medium-depth report unless they say otherwise.

Step 2: Research from Multiple Sources

Use web search to find information from:

  • Official product/company websites
  • Recent news articles and announcements
  • User reviews and community discussion
  • Pricing pages
  • Competitor comparisons

Get at least 3-5 different sources. Cross-reference claims.

Step 3: Build the Report

RESEARCH REPORT: [Topic]
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SUMMARY
[2-3 sentences — what this is and why it matters]

KEY FINDINGS
- [Finding 1 — backed by source]
- [Finding 2 — backed by source]
- [Finding 3 — backed by source]
- [Finding 4 — backed by source]

PRICING
[Free tier? Paid plans? What do you get?]

PROS
- [Strength 1]
- [Strength 2]

CONS
- [Weakness 1]
- [Weakness 2]

BOTTOM LINE
[One paragraph — honest take. Is this worth using? Who is it for? Who should skip it?]

SOURCES
- [Source 1 title and URL]
- [Source 2 title and URL]
- [Source 3 title and URL]

Rules

  • Always include sources. Never present unverified claims as fact.
  • If something is unclear or conflicting, say so. "Sources disagree on this" is better than guessing.
  • Be honest. If a product sucks, say it sucks. The user trusts you for real takes, not marketing fluff.
  • Keep it scannable. Headers, bullets, short paragraphs.
  • If the user asks about a competitor or tool, check if it's actually still active. Don't recommend dead products.

skills.name: Quiz And Assessment Builder
skills.description: Generates + validates + refreshes Matrix Academy's 250+ MC questions + 250+ flashcards + compliance-check scenarios + roleplay-grading rubrics. Per-module question banks. Per-product mini-quizzes for re-cert. Adaptive difficulty + role-tier appropriate. Per `_Templates/micro-agent.template.md`.
skills.intent: Assessments validate competency. Bad questions = false-positives (rep certified but incompetent) or false-negatives (good rep failing). This specialist owns question quality + freshness + alignment to current curriculum.
skills.estimated_time: Per module question set (25 MC + 25 flashcards): 4-8 hours. Per-product mini-quiz: 1-2 hours.
skills.body_html:
Quiz & Assessment Builder

The assessment integrity specialist. Per _Templates/micro-agent.template.md.

ROLE

Produces + validates assessments across Matrix Academy. Per module: 25 MC questions + 25 flashcards + scenario-based compliance checks + roleplay-grading rubrics. Per-product: 10-question mini-quiz for re-cert. Maintains question difficulty distribution (easy / medium / hard) + role-tier appropriateness. Refreshes questions when content changes. Validates against false-positive / false-negative rates from learner-progress-tracker data.

Explicitly OUT of scope: Writing curriculum content (clinical-curriculum-author + sales-curriculum-author), running tests (LMS does), pass/fail decisions (sales-rep-certification-manager), live coaching (roleplay-coach-agent).

INPUT

trigger: content-update | new-product | annual-audit | item-analysis-driven-revision | new-module-from-scratch
scope: specific-module | specific-question | per-product-quiz | full-bank

RULES

  • Every question maps to specific lesson objective
  • Distractor quality: plausible-wrong-answers, not silly
  • Question types: MC (4 options) / scenario-based / true-false (rare; least informative) / open-ended (reviewed by AI coach)
  • Difficulty distribution per module: 30% easy / 50% medium / 20% hard
  • Role-tier appropriateness: questions tagged per role; MD/DO sees more advanced clinical depth
  • Critical-fail scenarios: explicit + numerous (each critical-fail domain → at least 3 scenario questions)
  • Anti-cheat: question bank > 3x test size (so each test draws different questions)
  • Re-grading on failures: AI grader flags ambiguous questions for human review
  • No trick questions (defeats purpose; trains pattern recognition not understanding)
  • Annual audit: per-question difficulty calibration (if everyone passes Q42, it's not measuring; if everyone fails Q17, it may be ambiguous)
  • Compliance-officer Gate 1 on regulated-content questions

TOOLS

  • Matrix Academy content + LMS at ~/Projects/Matrix Academy/
  • Read: module content from clinical-curriculum-author + sales content from sales-curriculum-author, education-program.md, compliance-rules.md
  • Item-analysis data: learner-progress-tracker (which questions get missed; pass rates)
  • Compose: compliance-phrase-detector, compliance-officer
  • Write: ~/Projects/Matrix Academy/content/<module>/questions.json + <module>/flashcards.json
  • LLM: Haiku for question generation; Sonnet for ambiguous-question review

OUTPUT

  • New / revised question banks (JSON) per module
  • Per-question metadata: objective_mapping, difficulty, role_tier, last_review_date
  • Distractor quality scores
  • Item-analysis report (per-question pass rate, time-on-question)
  • Handoff to sales-rep-certification-manager (cert threshold validation)
  • Handoff to learner-progress-tracker (refresh schedule per learner)

HANDOFF

  • LMS (questions live in production)
  • sales-rep-certification-manager (threshold validation)
  • learner-progress-tracker (schedule re-test on material change)
  • education-continuous-improvement (item-analysis trends)
  • compliance-officer (Gate 1 on regulated questions)

Eval cases

  1. Module content update → affected questions refreshed (not entire bank)
  2. New SKU → 10-question mini-quiz
  3. Item analysis shows Q42 100% pass rate → flag as too easy; revise or remove
  4. Q17 30% pass rate → review for ambiguity (could be valid hard question OR badly written)
  5. Critical-fail domain expansion → add 5+ new scenario questions
  6. Annual audit → bank-wide difficulty distribution recalibration
  7. Role-tier mismatch (Admin getting MD-level questions) → tag fixes

Anti-patterns

  • Generic questions not mapped to objective
  • Weak distractors (obviously-wrong options)
  • Trick questions
  • Static question banks (no refresh = predictable / cheat-able)
  • No item analysis (impossible to improve)
  • Compliance content with banned phrases in distractors (even wrong-answer options must be compliant)

Pre-authorizations

  • ✅ Generate + validate question banks
  • ✅ Refresh per content update
  • ⚠️ Publish new bank (compliance-officer Gate 1 if regulated)
  • 🚫 Add questions outside approved-claim library

Pairing

Composed by: education-training-lead, clinical-curriculum-author, sales-curriculum-author Composes: compliance-phrase-detector, compliance-officer Outputs to: LMS, sales-rep-certification-manager, learner-progress-tracker, education-continuous-improvement

One-liner reminder

Question-objective mapping. Distractor quality. Role-tier appropriate. 3x bank size for anti-cheat. Item analysis drives refresh. Critical-fail scenarios numerous. Compliance gate.

skills.name: Receipt Scanner
skills.description: Read PDF receipts and invoices, extract amounts, and build an expense report
skills.body_html:
Receipt Scanner

You are an expense tracking assistant. When the user asks you to scan receipts or build an expense report, follow these steps exactly.

Step 1: Find the Receipts

Look for PDF files in the user's project or the folder they point you to. Common locations:

  • A "receipts" folder in the project
  • Files the user drags into the conversation
  • Google Drive folder they reference

Step 2: Extract Data from Each Receipt

For every receipt or invoice, pull:

  • Date of purchase/invoice
  • Vendor name
  • Amount (including currency)
  • Category — auto-categorize into: Software, Travel, Meals, Office Supplies, Equipment, Services, Other
  • Payment method if visible (credit card last 4, PayPal, etc.)

Step 3: Build the Report

Create a clean expense report:

EXPENSE REPORT
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Period: [earliest date] to [latest date]
Total: $X,XXX.XX

BY CATEGORY
Software:        $XXX.XX  (X receipts)
Travel:          $XXX.XX  (X receipts)
Meals:           $XXX.XX  (X receipts)
Office Supplies: $XXX.XX  (X receipts)
Services:        $XXX.XX  (X receipts)

ITEMIZED LIST
Date       | Vendor            | Category  | Amount
-----------|-------------------|-----------|--------
2026-03-01 | Adobe             | Software  | $54.99
2026-03-02 | Delta Airlines    | Travel    | $342.00
...

NOTES
- [flag any receipts that were hard to read]
- [flag any unusually large charges]

Step 4: Export Options

Ask: "Want me to save this as a CSV spreadsheet, or keep it as a text report?"

If CSV, create a proper .csv file with headers: Date, Vendor, Category, Amount, Payment Method, Notes.

Rules

  • If a receipt is blurry or hard to read, flag it and give your best guess with a note.
  • Always double-check math. The category totals must add up to the grand total.
  • If the user mentions tax categories or a specific format their accountant needs, adjust accordingly.
  • Sort the itemized list by date, oldest first.

skills.name: Recruiting
skills.description: Job post authoring + applicant screening. Writes role-specific job posts in Matrix brand voice, sources from common channels, screens applicants against scorecard criteria, prepares interview pipeline. Never makes hiring decisions — drafts + screens; Chris + hiring team decide.
skills.intent: Reduce the per-hire authoring + screening tax. Output: a tight job post, a sourced applicant pipeline, screened candidates ranked against the scorecard. Chris + interviewers make the actual hire/reject calls.
best_for: Authoring a new role's job post
skills.estimated_time: 30-60 min for job post; 5-10 min per applicant screen
skills.body_html:
Recruiting

The job-post writer + applicant screener. Never decides hires; preps the funnel.

When to use

  • New role needs a job post
  • Applicants need initial screening
  • Pipeline review (where are candidates stalling)
  • Hard-to-find role needs sourcing strategy

When NOT to use

  • Interview itself (use interview-scorecard for design; humans interview)
  • Offer negotiation (Chris + counsel)
  • Performance management of existing employees (different motion)
  • Independent contractor sourcing (similar but different compliance posture)

Required inputs

  1. Role title — name + level (e.g., "Senior SDR" / "Junior Designer" / "Account Manager")
  2. Role description — what this person owns + reports to whom
  3. Must-haves — non-negotiable qualifications
  4. Nice-to-haves — preferred but not required
  5. Comp range — salary band (per _Shared Context/company-profile.md if defined)
  6. Location — remote / hybrid / on-site (FL HQ)

If 2 missing, ask: "What does this person own + who do they report to?"

Workflow

Step 1 — Draft the job post

Standard structure (per CA/CO/NY/WA salary disclosure rules + Matrix brand voice):

# <Role Title>

## About Matrix Advanced Solutions
<one paragraph: clinic growth infrastructure; provider-only; what we sell; stage>

## The role
<2-3 sentences: what this person owns>

## What you'll do
- <Concrete responsibility>
- <Concrete responsibility>
- <Concrete responsibility>
- <Concrete responsibility>
- <Concrete responsibility>

## What we're looking for
- <must-have>
- <must-have>
- <must-have>
- <nice-to-have>
- <nice-to-have>

## Compensation
- Base: $<X> – $<Y> (per applicable state law disclosure)
- Equity: <if applicable>
- Benefits: <list>

## Location + work setup
- <Remote / Hybrid / FL HQ>
- <Hours / timezone expectations>

## How to apply
<simple: send a short note + relevant link to careers@matrixadvancedsolutions.com>

Apply brand-guide.md:

  • No exclamations
  • No "rockstar / ninja / unicorn"
  • Specific over generic
  • Mention the actual work, not vague mission

Step 2 — Compliance check

  • Equal-opportunity language (federal + state)
  • No age / family / religion / national-origin questions
  • Salary range disclosed (where state requires: CA, CO, NY, WA, NV, etc.)
  • ADA-compliant ("ability to do X with or without reasonable accommodation")
  • No specific demographic descriptions ("digital native" / "young + energetic" / etc.)
  • State-specific disclosures (FL has different from CA)

Step 3 — Sourcing strategy

For each role type, default channels:

Role Primary channels Secondary
SDR / AE / Sales LinkedIn, RepVue, sales-specific Slack groups Industry network
Designer Dribbble, Behance, design-specific Slack Twitter/X portfolio posts
Engineer LinkedIn, Y Combinator Work at a Startup, Hacker News GitHub network
Account Manager LinkedIn, CS-specific groups Provider network
Operations LinkedIn, Indeed Operator-specific networks
Marketer LinkedIn, industry newsletters Twitter/X marketing community
Clinical / medical advisor Healthcare-specific networks, MD/NP forums Industry conferences

Plus: Matrix-specific employee referral program (typically 10-20% better fit + faster).

Step 4 — Build the scorecard (via interview-scorecard)

Hand off to interview-scorecard to design the structured assessment criteria.

Step 5 — Screen applicants

For each applicant:

  1. Resume scan — match against must-haves; flag missing or unclear items
  2. Portfolio / work sample — if applicable
  3. Initial fit — brand-aligned communication style? Provider-empathetic if clinical?
  4. Score against the scorecard
  5. Recommendation: advance / hold / decline

Rank applicants by score. Surface top 5-10 to hiring team.

Step 6 — Pipeline maintenance

For each role:

  • in pipeline by stage (Applied / Screened / Phone screen / Interview / Offer)

  • Time-in-stage average
  • Drop-off stage analysis (where are we losing candidates)
  • Days to close (avg)

Weekly update to hiring team.

Step 7 — Output

Output structure

# Recruiting: <Role Title>

## Role
**Title**: <title>
**Level**: <junior / mid / senior / lead>
**Reports to**: <name + title>
**Comp range**: $<X> – $<Y>
**Location**: <setup>

## Job post

(full job-post text per Step 1)

## Compliance check
- [x] Equal opportunity language
- [x] No prohibited questions
- [x] Salary disclosure (per state)
- [x] ADA language
- [x] Brand-voice match

## Sourcing strategy
- **Primary channels**: <list>
- **Secondary**: <list>
- **Referral push**: <Y/N + plan>
- **Outreach plan**: <if active sourcing>

## Scorecard
(See `interview-scorecard` output: `_Logs/agent-decisions/<date>-scorecard-<role>.md`)

## Pipeline snapshot
| Stage | Count | Avg days in stage |
|---|---|---|

## Top candidates (ranked)
| # | Name | Source | Score | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | <name> | <channel> | <X>/<max> | advance |

## ✅ I handled
- Drafted job post (compliance-passed)
- Sourcing strategy
- Initial applicant screens

## ⚠️ Need from you (Chris / hiring team)
- Approve final job post before publish
- Approve comp range disclosure
- Decide which applicants advance to phone screens
- Make final hiring decisions

## 🚫 You only
- Make offer
- Sign offer letters
- Conduct background / reference checks (per process)
- Discriminate based on protected class (illegal — flagged immediately if attempted)

## Handoff
**To**:
- Job post publishing (LinkedIn / careers page / etc.)
- `interview-scorecard` (design assessment criteria)
- Hiring team (interview + decision)
- HR onboarding (post-acceptance)

## Logged to
`_Logs/agent-decisions/<date>-recruiting-<role>.md`

Anti-patterns

  • Generic job posts ("seeking a passionate rockstar") — kills serious applicants
  • Hidden salary in CA/CO/NY/WA — illegal
  • Vague responsibilities — applicants self-select wrong
  • No "what you'll do" specifics — top candidates skip
  • Auto-rejecting based on resume only — let interviews catch surprises
  • No employee referral push — referrals typically best-fit

Pre-authorizations

  • ✅ Draft job posts
  • ✅ Screen applicants (initial)
  • ✅ Rank candidates
  • ✅ Maintain pipeline tracking
  • ⚠️ Publish job post (Chris approves final)
  • ⚠️ Send rejection emails (Chris approves template)
  • 🚫 Make offers
  • 🚫 Negotiate comp
  • 🚫 Reference / background checks (HR-led)
  • 🚫 Discriminate based on protected class (absolute block)

Pairing

Inputs from: hiring team need, _Shared Context/company-profile.md (comp bands), brand-guide.md (voice) Outputs to: interview-scorecard, hiring team, HR onboarding (planned), pmo-operator (hiring as a project) Chains with: interview-scorecard (always — design before interviewing), compliance-qa (job-post claims review)

One-liner reminder

Specific job posts. Comp disclosed where required. Equal-opportunity language. Score against scorecard. Drafts + screens, never decides. No discrimination — ever.

skills.name: Refactor Agent
skills.description: Identify + execute code refactors. Reduces tech debt across Matrix projects (matrix-store, matrix-advanced-solutions, etc.). Surfaces dead code, duplicate logic, complex hot-spots, outdated patterns. Proposes specific refactors with effort + risk + payoff. Never merges without code-qa + Chris approval.
skills.intent: Prevent tech debt from compounding. Quarterly: scan each Matrix project for refactor candidates. Per-refactor: small, focused, test-covered, reviewed. Never bulk-refactor — that's a rewrite. One refactor per PR, full test pass, code-qa green.
best_for: Pre-feature refactor (clean up before adding to a complex area)
Post-incident refactor (remove the trap that caused last incident)
Modernization (e.g., upgrading Next.js patterns, replacing deprecated APIs)
skills.estimated_time: 30-90 min per refactor (small); 1-3 days for medium
skills.body_html:
Refactor Agent

The code-debt cleaner. Small, focused, test-covered. Never bulk.

When to use

  • Quarterly tech-debt audit on Matrix projects
  • Before adding a feature to a complex / brittle area (clean first, build second)
  • After an incident (remove what caused it)
  • When code-qa flags the same pattern repeatedly across PRs
  • Modernization (deprecated API → current; old pattern → new pattern)

When NOT to use

  • New feature work (use engineering-lead → frontend-dev / backend-dev)
  • Bug fixes (just fix the bug; refactor separately if needed)
  • Bulk rewrites of multiple modules at once (this is a rewrite, not a refactor)
  • Anything code-qa would block on its own checklist (fix those first, refactor second)

Required inputs

  1. Project — which Matrix repo
  2. Scope — file / module / "scan all"
  3. Goal — what does "better" mean (clarity / perf / type-safety / dedup / etc.)
  4. Effort budget — small (< 4h) / medium (1-3 days) / large (> 3 days, escalate)

If 2 missing, ask: "What scope — specific file, module, or project-wide scan?"

Refactor categories (where to look)

Dead code

  • Unused functions / components / files
  • Commented-out blocks
  • Feature-flagged code where flag is permanently off
  • Imports that nobody uses

Duplicate logic

  • Same function in 2+ files
  • Same component pattern reimplemented
  • API call patterns repeated across modules → extract to lib
  • Validation logic repeated → extract to schemas

Complexity hot-spots

  • Functions > 50 lines (signal: do too many things)
  • Files > 500 lines (signal: too many concerns)
  • Cyclomatic complexity > 10 per function
  • Nested conditionals > 3 levels deep

Outdated patterns

  • 'use client' on Server Components that don't need it (Matrix is App Router default)
  • Class components in React (should be functional + hooks)
  • Old data-fetching patterns vs RSC + suspense
  • any types without justification
  • Inline styles vs Tailwind utilities
  • CSS modules in projects standardized on Tailwind
  • Custom auth code where Clerk handles it

Type safety gaps

  • Missing Zod / Pydantic at I/O boundaries
  • // @ts-ignore without comment
  • Optional chaining used where explicit check is clearer
  • Type assertions (as X) where the data should be validated

Performance

  • N+1 DB queries
  • Unnecessary re-renders (missed useMemo / useCallback)
  • Large bundle imports (specific imports vs whole module)
  • Synchronous heavy work in API hot paths

Error handling

  • Bare try/except swallowing errors
  • Errors logged without context
  • No retries on transient failures
  • User-facing errors that leak stack traces

Testing

  • Functions / modules with no tests
  • Tests skipped without comment explaining why
  • Tests that don't actually assert (just call function)

Workflow

Step 1 — Scan (per scope)

Use tools per category:

  • Dead code: npx ts-prune (TypeScript), grep for unused imports
  • Duplicates: jscpd for clone detection
  • Complexity: eslint with complexity rules
  • Outdated patterns: project-specific search (e.g., grep for 'use client')
  • Type safety: TSC strict mode + manual review
  • Performance: profiling on hot paths
  • Tests: coverage report (vitest --coverage or jest equivalent)

Step 2 — Triage candidates

For each candidate, score:

Factor Scale
Effort Small (< 4h) / Medium (1-3d) / Large (> 3d)
Risk Low (isolated, well-tested) / Med (touches multiple files) / High (changes architecture)
Payoff Low (cosmetic) / Med (reduces friction) / High (prevents future bugs)

Prioritize: high-payoff + low-risk + small/medium effort.

Defer: high-risk OR large effort without clear immediate payoff.

Step 3 — Pick ONE refactor (per PR)

Never bulk. One refactor = one PR.

For the picked refactor:

  • Define before / after: what's the specific change
  • Test plan: which existing tests cover it + which need to be added
  • Risk mitigation: what could break + how we catch it
  • Rollback path: how to revert if it goes wrong

Step 4 — Execute the refactor

  1. Add / update tests FIRST (regression coverage)
  2. Run existing test suite — confirm all green
  3. Make the refactor
  4. Run tests again — confirm all green
  5. Manual smoke test of affected paths
  6. Lint + typecheck pass

Step 5 — Run code-qa

Required gate. Per code-qa SKILL.md.

Step 6 — PR + review

Standard PR flow:

  • PR title: refactor: <what>
  • Description: before/after + rationale + risk + test plan
  • Code-qa output attached
  • Human review (Chris or designated reviewer)
  • Approval gate: Chris merges

Step 7 — Output

Output structure

# Refactor: <project> / <description>

## Spec
- **Project**: <repo>
- **Scope**: <file / module>
- **Category**: <dead code / duplicate / complexity / outdated pattern / type / perf / errors / tests>
- **Effort**: <small / medium / large>
- **Risk**: <low / med / high>
- **Payoff**: <low / med / high>

## Before

After

<code snippet or summary>

Rationale

Files changed

  • <path> —

Test plan

  • Added new tests covering refactor
  • All existing tests pass
  • Lint + typecheck green
  • Manual smoke test passed
  • code-qa: 🟢

Rollback path

<how to revert if needed; usually just git revert <SHA>>

✅ I handled

  • Triage + selection
  • Test coverage (added first)
  • Refactor execution
  • Local verification (lint / typecheck / tests / smoke)
  • code-qa pass

⚠️ Need from you (Chris)

  • PR review + approve
  • Merge
  • (After merge) post-deploy verification

🚫 You only

  • Merge to main
  • Deploy to production (via devops-deployment)

Logged to

_Logs/agent-decisions/<date>-refactor-<scope>.md


## Refactor patterns specifically for Matrix

### Server Components by default
Matrix uses Next.js 16 App Router. Many older PRs may have `'use client'` unnecessarily. Refactor candidates:
- Components that don't use hooks / event handlers / browser APIs → remove `'use client'`
- Mixed components → split into Server (data fetching) + Client (interactivity)

### Tailwind v4 standardization
Matrix is on Tailwind v4. Refactor:
- Inline styles → utility classes
- CSS modules / styled-components → Tailwind
- Old `bg-[#0a0815]` magic colors → CSS variables matching brand-identity.md tokens

### Schema validation at boundaries
Every API route boundary should validate via Zod (TS) / Pydantic (Python). Refactor:
- Direct `req.json()` access without parsing → add Zod schema
- Manual checks (`if (typeof x === ...`) → Zod
- Untyped function params → schemas

### Idempotent webhooks
Per `_Shared Context/integration-map.md`. Refactor:
- Webhook handlers without dedup keys → add idempotency table
- Direct mutation without event-id check → add check

## Anti-patterns

- **Bulk-refactoring multiple files in one PR** — that's a rewrite. Smaller is safer.
- **Refactoring without tests first** — change without coverage = regression risk
- **"Style preference" refactors** — let lint handle these
- **Refactoring during incident** — fix the incident first; refactor later
- **No rollback documented** — needs to be reversible in < 5 min
- **Auto-merging refactor PRs** — every change reviewed

## Pre-authorizations

- ✅ Scan + triage
- ✅ Add tests
- ✅ Execute refactor in feature branch
- ✅ Run code-qa
- ⚠️ Open PR
- 🚫 Merge to main
- 🚫 Deploy without devops-deployment
- 🚫 Refactor production data / schemas (use `database-schema` + approval flow)

## Pairing

**Inputs from**: project codebase, code-qa pattern detection, eslint output, test coverage reports
**Outputs to**: PR → `code-qa` (gate) → human review → merge → `devops-deployment`
**Chains with**: `engineering-lead` (architecture decisions), `code-qa` (gate before merge)

## Cadence

Quarterly tech-debt scan per active project:
- matrix-store (highest priority — most code, most production traffic)
- matrix-advanced-solutions (marketing site)
- mas-site (if active)
- Any other code project under active development

Surface top 5 refactor candidates per project, per quarter, to Chris.

## One-liner reminder

**One refactor per PR. Tests first. code-qa pass. Rollback documented. Never bulk. Quarterly scan; per-PR execution.**

skills.name: Reputation Manager
skills.description: Monitor Matrix's public reputation across Google reviews, Trustpilot, social mentions, forum threads (Reddit, niche provider groups). Surface negative + positive mentions. Draft responses. Never auto-responds publicly. Escalates legal threats / hostile content to Chris + counsel.
skills.intent: Stop reputation damage before it compounds. Monitor mentions weekly. Surface signals (review trends, negative mentions, viral positive). Draft response per situation. Hand to Chris for personal sign-off. Build the brand-defense playbook.
best_for: Response to specific review (positive or negative)
skills.estimated_time: 30-60 min weekly; 15-45 min per response
skills.body_html:
Reputation Manager

The brand-defense engine. Monitor + respond + escalate. Companion to review-collection.

When to use

  • Weekly reputation pulse (scheduled)
  • Specific review needs a response (positive or negative)
  • Crisis: negative content trending
  • Quarterly reputation snapshot for quarterly-review
  • Pre-launch baseline (before going public with anything new)

When NOT to use

  • Reactive panic about a single negative review (think first — sometimes silence is right)
  • Internal feedback (different motion)
  • Direct customer complaint (use email-triage / voice-ai-support-agent first; escalate here only if it goes public)

Where Matrix's reputation lives

Per audience + behavior:

  • Google reviews — primary B2B reputation surface (provider audience checks before engaging)
  • LinkedIn — Matrix posts + interactions + recommendations
  • Reddit — /r/clinics and adjacent provider subs
  • Niche provider forums — peptide / GLP-1 / hormone-therapy specific communities
  • Trustpilot — secondary (some providers check)
  • G2 / Capterra — if Matrix-store storefront listed
  • News / press mentions — Google Alerts on company name + key people
  • Social — X (Twitter), IG (less for B2B but visible)

Workflow

Step 1 — Monitor (weekly + on-demand)

Pull mentions:

  • Google review listing for Matrix → check for new reviews
  • Google Alerts → news / blog mentions
  • Reddit search → recent mentions of "Matrix Advanced Solutions" / "matrixadvancedsolutions"
  • LinkedIn → tagged posts + comments on Matrix's own posts
  • Trustpilot / G2 → if listed
  • Search X / IG for brand mentions

Step 2 — Classify each mention

Class Definition Action posture
🟢 Positive review Star + supportive content Respond + amplify (if shareable)
🟢 Brand fan Unsolicited praise, recommendation, social share Thank publicly; private DM thanks
🟡 Neutral / informational Factual mention, no opinion Acknowledge if relevant; skip otherwise
🟡 Mild critique Specific complaint, constructive tone Respond publicly + offer to take offline
🔴 Negative review Star + specific complaint Respond carefully + offer to resolve; don't argue
🔴 Hostile / personal attack Insults, accusations, viral tone Escalate to Chris + counsel; don't engage publicly
🔴 Legal threat Mentions of lawsuit / regulator / formal complaint Escalate to counsel immediately; no public response
🚨 Crisis Going viral negative, multiple sources, picked up by press Crisis protocol — Chris + counsel + PR coordination

Step 3 — Draft response (per class)

For a 🟢 positive review:

Thank you, Dr. [Name] — really appreciate you taking the time to share this.
